## NIST-CSWP-36A

- **Reference ID**: NIST-CSWP-36A
- **Title**: Protecting Subscriber Identifiers with Subscription Concealed Identifier (SUCI): Applying 5G Cybersecurity and Privacy Capabilities
- **Authors**: Michael Bartock; Jeffrey Cichonski; Murugiah Souppaya (NIST); Karen Scarfone (Scarfone Cybersecurity); Parisa Grayeli; Sanjeev Sharma; Charles Teague (MITRE)
- **Publication Date**: 2024-08-01
- **Last Updated**: 2024-08-01
- **Document Status**: Published
- **Main Topic**: This white paper describes the Subscription Concealed Identifier (SUCI) mechanism in 5G networks to protect subscriber identifiers using public-key encryption.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: None detected
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: United States; National Cybersecurity Center of Excellence (NCCoE); Federal Communications Commission (FCC)
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Michael Bartock, Jeffrey Cichonski, Murugiah Souppaya, Karen Scarfone, Parisa Grayeli, Sanjeev Sharma, Charles Teague, Muthukkumaran Ramalingam, Stefano Righi, Jitendra Patel, Bogdan Ungureanu, Tao Wan, Matt Hyatt, Steve Vetter, Dan Carroll, Steve Orrin, Corey Piggott, Michael Yeh, Sallie Edwards, Mary Raguso, Cherilyn Pascoe, Adam Sedgewick, Kevin Stine, Gary Atkinson, Rajasekhar Bodanki, Don McBride, Aarin Buskirk, Bryan Wenger, Todd Gibson
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: 3GPP TS 33.501, ECIES
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Unified Data Management (UDM), Subscription Identifier De-concealing Function (SIDF)
- **Standardization Bodies**: 3GPP, NIST
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: X25519, P-256, ECIES
- **Key Takeaways**: Network operators should enable SUCI with a non-null encryption cipher scheme to prevent IMSI-catching attacks; SUCI protects subscriber identifiers only on 5G networks and offers no protection on 4G LTE; Roaming scenarios may expose clear text network operator information even when the permanent identifier is encrypted; Emergency service requests may bypass access authorization and reveal the SUPI in clear text.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: Profile A, Profile B
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Support**: None detected

- **Authors**: NIST

## NIST-SP-800-131A-Rev3

- **Reference ID**: NIST-SP-800-131A-Rev3
- **Title**: Transitioning the Use of Cryptographic Algorithms and Key Lengths (Rev 3)
- **Authors**: NIST
- **Publication Date**: 2024-11-01
- **Last Updated**: 2024-11-01
- **Document Status**: Initial Public Draft
- **Main Topic**: NIST guidance on transitioning cryptographic algorithms and key lengths, including retirement schedules for SHA-1 and weak RSA/ECC keys aligned with PQC adoption.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: None detected
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Post-Quantum, Quantum Computer
- **Migration Timeline Info**: SHA-1 and RSA/ECC <128-bit disallowed after 2030; transition to quantum-resistant algorithms for digital signatures and key establishment post-2030.
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: United States; NIST
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Elaine Barker (NIST); Allen Roginsky (NIST)
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: HTTPS
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Key Management
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: FIPS 203; FIPS 204; FIPS 205
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: SHA-1; DSA; ECB; RSA; ECC; 224-bit hash functions
- **Key Takeaways**: Retirement of ECB as a confidentiality mode is proposed; SHA-1 and 224-bit hash functions have a defined retirement schedule; Transition from 112-bit to 128-bit security strength is required; Federal systems must adopt quantum-resistant algorithms for signatures and key establishment after 2030.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: 112 bits; 128 bits; 224-bit hash functions

## NIST-SP-800-133r3-ipd

- **Reference ID**: NIST-SP-800-133r3-ipd
- **Title**: SP 800-133 Rev. 3 (IPD): Recommendation for Cryptographic Key Generation
- **Authors**: NIST; Quynh Dang; Dustin Moody; Andrew Regenscheid; Hamilton Silberg
- **Publication Date**: 2026-04-17
- **Last Updated**: 2026-04-17
- **Document Status**: Initial Public Draft
- **Main Topic**: Guidance for generating cryptographic keys for use with approved algorithms. Rev. 3 integrates PQC key generation (ML-KEM, ML-DSA), introduces KEM-based symmetric key establishment, and expands seed-expansion methods via SHAKE and DRBGs. Aligns randomness guidance with SP 800-90C.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM (FIPS 203), ML-DSA (FIPS 204); seed-based deterministic key generation using SHAKE-128/256 and SP 800-90A DRBGs
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Harvest-now-decrypt-later (HNDL) risk mitigated by transitioning key generation to PQC algorithms; classical asymmetric key generation (RSA, ECDH) is quantum-vulnerable
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Public comment period closes June 16, 2026; final publication expected late 2026; applicable to federal agencies under FISMA and OMB Circular A-130
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: United States (federal); Global (advisory); NIST, U.S. Department of Commerce
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Quynh Dang (lead author); Dustin Moody (author); Andrew Regenscheid (author); Hamilton Silberg (author)
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: KEM-based key establishment; seed derivation for asymmetric key pairs; DRBG-based key generation
- **Infrastructure Layers**: HSM firmware; cryptographic libraries; key management systems; PKCS#11 tokens
- **Standardization Bodies**: National Institute of Standards and Technology
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: FIPS 203 (ML-KEM); FIPS 204 (ML-DSA); FIPS 186-5; SP 800-90A; SP 800-90B; SP 800-90C; SP 800-56C Rev. 2; SP 800-108 Rev. 1; FISMA 2014; OMB Circular A-130
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA; DSA; ECDH; ECDSA; DH; symmetric AES; Triple-DES (deprecated)
- **Key Takeaways**: First NIST SP 800-133 revision to include KEM-based symmetric key generation (ML-KEM); Asymmetric PQC key pairs (ML-DSA, ML-KEM) can now be generated deterministically from a seed using SHAKE or DRBG expansion; SP 800-90C alignment tightens entropy requirements for all key generation paths; Seed management becomes a critical security primitive — loss or compromise of a root seed exposes all derived keys; Hybrid key generation (classical + PQC from shared seed) is acknowledged but scoped to transition guidance.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ML-KEM-512 (L1), ML-KEM-768 (L3), ML-KEM-1024 (L5); ML-DSA-44 (L1), ML-DSA-65 (L3), ML-DSA-87 (L5)
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid key generation acknowledged for transition period; classical + PQC key pairs can be derived from a common seed for interoperability during migration
- **Pure PQC KEM Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Support**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Pure PQC Signature Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Support**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Covered**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Evidence**: None detected
- **Lifecycle State**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Seed-based deterministic generation reduces HSM entropy consumption; SHAKE expansion is computationally lightweight vs. full DRBG instantiation per key
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Developer, Compliance Officer, HSM Vendor, Cryptographic Module Developer
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: SP 800-90A/B/C-compliant entropy source; FIPS 203/204-capable cryptographic library; secure seed storage (HSM or equivalent)
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: entropy-randomness, hsm-pqc, kms-pqc, standards-bodies, algorithms
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: Root seed compromise exposes all derived keys; DRBG backtracking attacks if seed reused across instantiations; inadequate entropy source undermining key generation security
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: Key generation audit trails; inventory of asymmetric key generation methods in use; identify RSA/ECDH key generation for PQC migration
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: ACVP test vectors for ML-KEM and ML-DSA key generation; CMVP validation of DRBG and entropy source; known-answer tests (KATs) for seed-expansion paths
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: None detected
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: randomness extraction; DRBG seeding
- **Constrained Device & IoT Suitability**: Seed-based deterministic generation is suitable for constrained devices; reduces per-key entropy demands; SHAKE expansion preferred over full DRBG for resource-limited environments
- **Supply Chain & Vendor Risk**: HSM and crypto library vendors must update key generation APIs to support PQC seed expansion; CMVP re-validation required for modules implementing new methods
- **Deployment & Migration Complexity**: hybrid (i.e., combined classical and post-quantum) implementations
- **Financial & Business Impact**: CMVP re-validation costs for affected cryptographic modules; HSM firmware upgrade costs; potential timeline impact if final publication delayed past late 2026
- **Organizational Readiness**: Federal agencies must plan for SP 800-133 Rev. 3 adoption as part of PQC migration programs per OMB M-23-02; commercial organizations should align with timeline for FIPS 203/204 adoption
- **Math Family**: None detected
- **PQC Round**: None detected
- **Extraction Note**: carry-forward (DS05p2): record not iterated this run; preserved from prior enrichment

## NIST-SP-800-230-ipd

- **Reference ID**: NIST-SP-800-230-ipd
- **Title**: Additional SLH-DSA Parameter Sets for Limited-Signature Use Cases (Initial Public Draft)
- **Authors**: NIST
- **Publication Date**: 2026-04-13
- **Last Updated**: 2026-04-13
- **Document Status**: Initial Public Draft
- **Main Topic**: NIST SP 800-230 extends FIPS 205 with six new SLH-DSA parameter sets optimized for limited-signature use cases like firmware and software signing.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: SLH-DSA, SPHINCS+
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Public Comments Period: April 13, 2026 – June 12, 2026
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: Regions: United States; Bodies: National Institute of Standards and Technology, U.S. Department of Commerce, Office of Management and Budget
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Quynh Dang (Author); Dustin Moody (Author); John Kelsey (provided insights)
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: None detected
- **Infrastructure Layers**: None detected
- **Standardization Bodies**: National Institute of Standards and Technology
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: FIPS 205, Federal Information Security Modernization Act (FISMA) of 2014, OMB Circular A-130
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: SHA2, SHAKE
- **Key Takeaways**: Six new SLH-DSA parameter sets are defined for security levels 1, 3, and 5 with a strict limit of 2^24 signatures per key; These variants offer reduced signature sizes and faster verification compared to standard FIPS 205 sets; The parameter sets are not approved for general-purpose use due to the operational signature limit; Users must evaluate signing frequency to ensure the 2^24 limit is never exceeded during a key's lifetime.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: Security levels 1, 3, and 5; 2^24 signatures per signing key limit; SHA2 and SHAKE hash functions
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Support**: None detected

- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Evidence**: None detected
- **Lifecycle State**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Signatures are roughly half the size of FIPS 205 variants; Fast verification times; Strict limit of 2^24 signatures per signing key
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Developer, Compliance Officer, Researcher
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Thorough evaluation to ensure signature limit is never exceeded; Assessment of number of signing devices and frequency of signature generation

## NIST-SP-800-38D

- **Reference ID**: NIST-SP-800-38D
- **Title**: Recommendation for Block Cipher Modes of Operation: Galois/Counter Mode (GCM) and GMAC
- **Authors**: NIST
- **Publication Date**: 2007-11-01
- **Last Updated**: 2007-11-01
- **Document Status**: Final
- **Main Topic**: NIST Special Publication 800-38D specifies the Galois/Counter Mode (GCM) and GMAC as modes of operation for authenticated encryption using symmetric key block ciphers.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: None detected
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: United States; National Institute of Standards and Technology; U.S. Department of Commerce; Office of Management and Budget; Communications Security Establishment of the Government of Canada
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Morris Dworkin (Author); David McGrew (Co-inventor of GCM); Elaine Barker, John Kelsey, Allen Roginsky, Donna Dodson, Tim Polk, Bill Burr (Reviewers/Contributors)
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: None detected
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Cryptographic Module Validation Program; Key Establishment
- **Standardization Bodies**: National Institute of Standards and Technology; Communications Security Establishment of the Government of Canada
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: Federal Information Security Management Act (FISMA); OMB Circular A-130; FIPS Pub. 197; Cryptographic Module Validation Program (CMVP)
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: Advanced Encryption Standard (AES); Galois/Counter Mode (GCM); GMAC; Counter mode
- **Key Takeaways**: GCM provides authenticated encryption with associated data using a 128-bit block cipher; Nonce uniqueness per key is mandatory to prevent keystream recovery and tag forgery; GCM supports online processing where data lengths are not required in advance; Implementations must adhere to uniqueness requirements on initialization vectors to ensure security assurance; Conformance testing is conducted under the Cryptographic Module Validation Program framework
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: 128-bit block size; 96-bit (12-byte) nonce; 16-byte authentication tag; up to about 64 gigabytes per invocation for confidential data
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Support**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Pure PQC Signature Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Support**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Covered**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Evidence**: None detected
- **Lifecycle State**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Block size of 128 bits; Nonce length of 96 bits (12 bytes); Authentication tag length of 16 bytes; Confidential data limit of about 64 gigabytes per invocation
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Developer, Compliance Officer, Researcher
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Approved symmetric key block cipher with 128-bit block size; Unique initialization strings per key; Conformance to Cryptographic Module Validation Program requirements
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Algorithms, Compliance, tls-basics, entropy-randomness, code-signing

- **Reference ID**: NIST-SP-800-56C-R2
- **Title**: Recommendation for Key-Derivation Methods in Key-Establishment Schemes (Revision 2)
- **Authors**: NIST
- **Publication Date**: 2020-08-01
- **Last Updated**: 2020-08-01
- **Document Status**: Final
- **Main Topic**: Specification of one-step and extraction-then-expansion key-derivation methods for deriving keying material from shared secrets in key-establishment schemes.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: None explicitly listed; document supports hybrid Z′ = Z || T shared secret concatenation enabling PQC integration
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected (classical guidance with PQC-hybrid architecture accommodation)

- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: HMAC; AES-N-CMAC; hash function

- **Key Takeaways**: NSS owners must transition to CNSA 2.0 algorithms by 2035; software and firmware signing must use stateful signatures like LMS or XMSS immediately; RSA, DH, and ECC will be deprecated for NSS; Level V parameters are required for all classification levels; legacy equipment requires waivers if not refreshed regularly

- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid solutions may be allowed due to protocol standards, product availability, or interoperability requirements; CNSA 2.0 algorithms become mandatory at specific dates while CNSA 1.0 alone is no longer approved

## NSM-10

- **Reference ID**: NSM-10
- **Title**: National Security Memorandum 10 — Promoting U.S. Leadership in Quantum Computing
- **Authors**: White House
- **Publication Date**: 2022-05-04
- **Last Updated**: 2022-05-04
- **Document Status**: Final
- **Main Topic**: National Security Memorandum 10 directs U.S. federal agencies to inventory cryptographic systems and transition to quantum-resistant cryptography by 2035 while promoting leadership in quantum information science.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM, ML-DSA, SLH-DSA (NIST PQC standards mandated)
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: cryptanalytically relevant quantum computer (CRQC)
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Goal of mitigating quantum risk as much as feasible by 2035; first sets of standards expected by 2024; deprecation timeline proposed within 90 days of standard release with a goal to move systems off vulnerable cryptography within a decade.
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: Regions: United States; Bodies: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), National Security Agency (NSA), Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA), Office of Management and Budget (OMB), National Cyber Director, Office of Science and Technology Policy, Federal Civilian Executive Branch (FCEB) Agencies.
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: President Joe Biden; Vice President Kamala Harris; First Lady Dr. Jill Biden; Second Gentleman Douglas Emhoff; Secretary of Commerce; Secretary of Homeland Security; Director of NIST; Director of NSA; Director of CISA; Director of OMB; National Cyber Director; Assistant to the President for National Security Affairs (APNSA).
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: PKI, TLS, SSH, IPsec, national security communications
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Public-key cryptography; digital signatures; system administrator protocols; non-security software and firmware; High Value Assets; High Impact Systems.
- **Standardization Bodies**: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST); technical standards bodies.
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: National Quantum Initiative Act (Public Law 115-368); National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2022 (Public Law 117-81).
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: None detected
- **Key Takeaways**: Federal agencies must inventory all currently deployed cryptographic systems excluding National Security Systems; NIST and NSA are developing technical standards for quantum-resistant cryptography expected by 2024; The United States aims to mitigate quantum risk as much as feasible by 2035; Agencies must establish a Migration to Post-Quantum Cryptography Project at the National Cybersecurity Center of Excellence; Cryptographic agility is central to reducing transition time and allowing seamless updates.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: None detected
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: cryptographic agility; interoperable quantum-resistant cryptography; migration planning; inventorying vulnerable systems.

## OMB-M-23-02

- **Reference ID**: OMB-M-23-02
- **Title**: OMB Memorandum M-23-02 — Migrating to Post-Quantum Cryptography
- **Authors**: OMB; White House
- **Publication Date**: 2022-11-18
- **Last Updated**: 2022-11-18
- **Document Status**: Final
- **Main Topic**: OMB Memorandum M-23-02 directs federal agencies to conduct prioritized inventories of cryptographic systems vulnerable to cryptanalytically relevant quantum computers and outlines migration planning requirements.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM, ML-DSA, SLH-DSA (referenced as NIST PQC standards)
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Cryptanalytically relevant quantum computer (CRQC); Harvest Now Decrypt Later (encrypted data recorded now and later decrypted)
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Designate lead within 30 days; submit first inventory by May 4, 2023; annual inventories until 2035; goal to mitigate quantum risk by 2035
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: United States; Executive Departments and Agencies; Office of Management and Budget (OMB); Office of the National Cyber Director (ONCD); Department of Homeland Security Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA); National Security Agency (NSA); National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST); FedRAMP Program Management Office (PMO)
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Shalanda D. Young (Director, OMB); Federal Chief Information Security Officer (Chair of Cryptographic Migration Working Group)
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: PKI, TLS, SSH, IPsec
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Public Key Infrastructure; Cloud service providers; Continuous Diagnostics and Mitigation (CDM)
- **Standardization Bodies**: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: Federal Information Processing Standards (FIPS) 199; Federal Information Security Modernization Act (FISMA); Executive Order 14028; OMB Memorandum M-22-09; National Security Memorandum 10 (NSM-10)
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: None detected
- **Key Takeaways**: Agencies must submit a prioritized inventory of CRQC-vulnerable systems by May 4, 2023, and annually thereafter until 2035; Inventories must focus on High Value Assets (HVAs), high impact systems, and data expected to remain mission-sensitive in 2035; Agencies are required to assess funding needs for PQC migration within 30 days of each annual inventory submission; Testing of pre-standardized PQC is encouraged in production environments alongside current algorithms; A cryptographic migration working group will be established to coordinate agency efforts
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: None detected

## OpenSSL-3.5.0-Release

- **Reference ID**: OpenSSL-3.5.0-Release
- **Title**: OpenSSL 3.5.0 — First Release with Native Post-Quantum Cryptography
- **Authors**: OpenSSL Project
- **Publication Date**: 2025-04-29
- **Last Updated**: 2025-04-29
- **Document Status**: Released
- **Main Topic**: OpenSSL 3.5.0 is the first release to include native support for ML-KEM, ML-DSA, SLH-DSA, and X25519MLKEM768 hybrid TLS without requiring the OQS provider.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM, ML-DSA, SLH-DSA
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected

- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected

- **Infrastructure Layers**: PKI, Key Management
- **Standardization Bodies**: None detected
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: FIPS 140-3, FIPS 203, FIPS 204, FIPS 205
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA, EC, ECX, DH, X25519, AES-256-CBC, DES-EDE3-CBC, SM2, PBMAC1, OCB
- **Key Takeaways**: OpenSSL 3.5.0 enables mainstream PQC adoption by natively supporting ML-KEM, ML-DSA, and SLH-DSA without the OQS provider; Default TLS configurations now prefer hybrid PQC KEM groups including X25519MLKEM768; The release introduces server-side QUIC support and improved TLS key establishment configurability; Security patch releases (3.5.1-3.5.6) address critical vulnerabilities in RSA KEM, CMS parsing, and PKCS#12 handling; FIPS provider updates include PCT on key generation and import for RSA, EC, and ECX.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ML-KEM-768, X25519MLKEM768
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid PQC KEM groups, X25519MLKEM768 hybrid TLS, multiple TLS keyshares

The FIPS provider in OpenSSL 3.x enables FIPS 140-3 compliant operation. ## PKCS11-V3-OASIS

- **Reference ID**: PKCS11-V3-OASIS
- **Title**: PKCS #11 Cryptographic Token Interface Standard Version 3.0
- **Authors**: OASIS PKCS11 Technical Committee
- **Publication Date**: 2020-06-01
- **Last Updated**: 2020-06-01
- **Document Status**: OASIS Standard
- **Main Topic**: This document defines the PKCS #11 Cryptographic Token Interface Base Specification Version 3.0, an API called Cryptoki for cryptographic tokens and HSMs.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: Quantum-resistant algorithm support structure through extended mechanism definitions (FIPS 203/204/205 ready), mechanism framework for PQC integration, post-quantum signature and key encapsulation support planned in profiles
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Post-Quantum (implicit through mechanism extensibility)
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Years mentioned: 2020
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: Regions: United States, Bodies: NIST
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Tony Cox (TC Chair); Robert Relyea (TC Chair); Chris Zimman (Editor); Dieter Bong (Editor); Robert Griffin (Previous Editor); Tim Hudson (Previous Editor)
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: Red Hat, Utimaco, various HSM vendors implementing PKCS#11 v3.0
- **Protocols Covered**: TLS 1.3; X.509; WTLS
- **Infrastructure Layers**: HSM; Cryptographic Token Interface; Key Management
- **Standardization Bodies**: OASIS
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: OASIS IPR Policy, RFC standards (via reference), X.509 certificate standards, HSM vendor compliance, PKCS#11 v2.40 (predecessor), PKCS#11 Profiles v3.0, PKCS#11 Mechanisms specifications (Current and Historical)
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA; HKDF; SP800_108_COUNTER_KDF; SP800_108_FEEDBACK_KDF; SP800_108_DOUBLE_PIPELINE_KDF
- **Key Takeaways**: PKCS #11 v3.0 adds specific KDF mechanisms for TLS 1.3 and NIST SP 800-108 derivation; The standard is essential for HSM-based QKD integration workflows where secrets are imported as non-extractable keys; Users must use updated header files from the GitHub repository due to flaws in the published Committee Specification; This specification supersedes PKCS #11 Version 2.40; Machine-readable content in plain text files prevails over prose narrative in case of discrepancy

## PROACT-2025-SCA-Lattice-PQC

- **Reference ID**: PROACT-2025-SCA-Lattice-PQC
- **Title**: Side-Channel and Fault Attacks on ML-KEM and ML-DSA (PROACT 2025)

- **Publication Date**: 2025-01-01
- **Last Updated**: 2025-01-01
- **Document Status**: Published
- **Main Topic**: Analysis of side-channel and fault-injection attacks on software implementations of ML-KEM and ML-DSA lattice-based algorithms.

- **Key Takeaways**: Unmasked implementations of ML-KEM and ML-DSA are vulnerable to single-trace key recovery; Masked and shuffled countermeasures can be targeted by fault-injection attacks; Both ML-KEM and ML-DSA rely on the hardness of the Learning With Errors (LWE) problem; Lattice-based cryptography is resistant to quantum computers solving integer factorization or discrete logarithm problems.

- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Harvest now, decrypt later (HNDL); quantum computer attacks on asymmetric encryption; future quantum capabilities
- **Migration Timeline Info**: NIST released first finalized standards in August 2024; quantum computer with sufficient power not expected for at least another decade
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: United Kingdom; Bodies: NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology), NCSC (National Cyber Security Centre), The Quantum Finance Boardroom

- **Key Takeaways**: Organizations should build crypto inventories and introduce crypto-agile architectures to allow algorithm replacement without major redesign; Sensitive data with long retention periods is exposed to Harvest Now Decrypt Later threats requiring immediate assessment; QKD is not endorsed for government/military or commercial replacement of public key solutions by NCSC UK due to lack of standardization and distance limitations; NIST finalized ML-KEM, ML-DSA, and SLH-DSA standards in August 2024 providing a foundation for enterprise planning; Previous NIST candidates like SIKE and Rainbow were broken by classical computers, highlighting the importance of rigorous validation

- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid classical-quantum crypto; Crypto-agile architectures; Migration from quantum-vulnerable public-key cryptography

- **Authors**: The Quantum Insider
- **Publication Date**: 2026-04-27
- **Last Updated**: 2026-04-27
- **Document Status**: Released
- **Main Topic**: Overview of the quantum security landscape, including threats like harvest-now-decrypt-later, PQC standardization, QKD, and organizational migration strategies.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM, ML-DSA, SLH-DSA, FN-DSA, CRYSTALS-Kyber, CRYSTALS-Dilithium, SPHINCS+, FALCON
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Harvest-Now-Decrypt-Later, Shor’s algorithm, Grover’s algorithm, fault-tolerant quantum computer
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Cryptographic transitions historically take 10-20 years; NSA deadlines 2025-2033 with full NSS quantum resistance by 2035; Cloudflare and Google target full migration by 2029; Apple integrated PQC in early 2024
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: Regions: United States, China, European Union, South Korea, Japan, Singapore; Bodies: NIST, NSA, CISA, ETSI, Federal Reserve, Vanderbilt’s Institute of National Security
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Doug Adams (described HNDL situation at Vanderbilt Quantum Forum); Charles Bennett and Gilles Brassard (proposed BB84 protocol in 1984)
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: PQ3 protocol, IonQ hardware, SEALSQ post-quantum semiconductors, BTQ Technologies quantum-resistant blockchain security, PQShield PQC implementations, ID Quantique QKD systems, Toshiba QKD systems, QuantumCTek QKD systems, Microsoft open-source PQC libraries
- **Protocols Covered**: TLS, VPN, HTTPS, BB84, PQ3
- **Infrastructure Layers**: PKI (implied via digital signatures/certificates context), Key Management (implied via key exchange), Cloud KMS (implied via Azure), embedded devices, IoT, automotive applications, blockchain
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST, ETSI
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: FIPS 203, FIPS 204, FIPS 205, FIPS 206, CNSA 2.0

- **Title**: Why 2026 Matters for Quantum Security
- **Authors**: The Quantum Insider
- **Publication Date**: 2026-04-28
- **Last Updated**: 2026-04-28
- **Document Status**: Released
- **Main Topic**: The document analyzes why 2026 is a pivotal year for quantum security, driven by reduced qubit estimates for breaking encryption, finalized NIST standards, and approaching government migration deadlines.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: HQC
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Harvest now, decrypt later; breaking modern encryption; practical attacks on RSA and elliptic curve cryptography
- **Migration Timeline Info**: NIST guidance suggests phasing out quantum-vulnerable algorithms after 2030 and disallowing them after 2035; NSA CNSA 2.0 requires quantum-safe systems by January 2027; Cloudflare and Google target 2029 for full post-quantum security
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: United States; NIST; FBI; CISA; NSA; UK’s National Cyber Security Centre; FS-ISAC
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Craig Gidney (estimated qubit requirements for RSA-2048 factoring); Martin Ekerå (estimated qubit requirements for RSA-2048 factoring); Luke Preskey (CRO, contact for Year of Quantum Security 2026); Mohib Ur Rehman (author)

- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA; RSA-2048; elliptic curve cryptography; secp256k1
- **Key Takeaways**: Organizations must begin cryptographic discovery and inventory immediately to understand exposure; Migration timelines are compressing due to reduced qubit estimates for breaking RSA and ECC; Regulatory deadlines such as NSA's 2027 requirement and NIST's 2030/2035 phases mandate action; Early adoption allows for controlled implementation while delayed action faces resource constraints and tighter timelines; The "harvest now, decrypt later" threat necessitates protecting data with long confidentiality horizons
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: 2048-bit RSA; 256-bit problem (elliptic curve); 105 qubits (Google Willow); 20 million physical qubits (2019 estimate); under 1 million physical qubits (2025 estimate); under 100,000 physical qubits (2026 QLDPC estimate); under 500,000 physical qubits (2026 ECC estimate); 1,200 logical qubits; 1,450 logical qubits; 90 million Toffoli gates; 70 million Toffoli gates

- **Reference ID**: RFC 3370

- **Authors**: IETF
- **Publication Date**: 2003-08-01
- **Last Updated**: 2003-08-01
- **Document Status**: Proposed Standard
- **Main Topic**: This document specifies the conventions for using several cryptographic algorithms, including RSA, DSA, Diffie-Hellman, SHA-1, MD5, Triple-DES, and RC2, with the Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS).
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: None detected

- **Key Takeaways**: Implementations supporting specified algorithms must adhere strictly to the conventions defined in this document; RSA PKCS #1 v1.5 is vulnerable to adaptive chosen ciphertext attacks in interactive applications, requiring mitigation via OAEP or oracle protection; Cryptographic algorithms weaken over time, necessitating modular implementations for easy algorithm updates; Key wrap algorithms for ciphers other than Triple-DES or RC2 must be separately defined; Private keys and encryption keys must be protected to prevent masquerade and content disclosure

- **Key Takeaways**: The AES Key Wrap algorithm uses AES as a primitive to securely encrypt plaintext keys with integrity information; The algorithm operates on 64-bit blocks and supports 128-bit, 192-bit, and 256-bit key-encryption keys; The specification includes test vectors for various key data and KEK size combinations; Security assertions regarding the algorithm are made by the US Government, not the document authors; The algorithm is designed to satisfy NIST Key Wrap requirements for data longer than the AES block size.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: 128-bit key; 192-bit key; 256-bit key; 64-bit blocks; 128-bit AES block size

## RFC 3560

- **Reference ID**: RFC 3560
- **Title**: Use of the RSASSA-PSS Signature Algorithm in Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS)
- **Authors**: IETF
- **Publication Date**: 2003-07-01
- **Last Updated**: 2003-07-01
- **Document Status**: Proposed Standard
- **Main Topic**: Specifies the conventions for using the RSAES-OAEP key transport algorithm with the Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) to address vulnerabilities in PKCS #1 Version 1.5.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: None detected
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: R. Housley (Author); Burt Kaliski, Jakob Jonsson, Francois Rousseau, and Jim Schaad (Acknowledged for contributions)
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS); S/MIME; Secure Sockets Layer (SSL); Transport Layer Security (TLS)
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Key Management; Certificate-based key management; PKIX
- **Standardization Bodies**: IETF; Internet Society; IANA; NIST
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA; RSAES-OAEP; Triple-DES (3DES); SHA-1; SHA-2
- **Key Takeaways**: Adopt RSAES-OAEP instead of PKCS #1 Version 1.5 to mitigate adaptive chosen ciphertext attacks like the Million Message Attack; RSA public keys used for RSAES-OAEP must be at least 1024 bits in length; Implementations must adjust parity bits for Triple-DES content-encryption keys to ensure odd parity prior to RSAES-OAEP encryption; RSAES-OAEP is recommended for both store-and-forward and interactive request-response environments for long-term security assurance.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: 1024-bit RSA minimum key length
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Support**: None detected

- **Implementation Attack Surface**: adaptive chosen ciphertext attacks; Million Message Attack

## RFC 4251

- **Reference ID**: RFC 4251
- **Title**: The Secure Shell (SSH) Protocol Architecture
- **Authors**: Tatu Ylonen; Chris M. Lonvick (Ed.)
- **Publication Date**: 2006-01-01
- **Last Updated**: 2006-01-01
- **Document Status**: Proposed Standard
- **Main Topic**: Defines the architectural framework of the Secure Shell (SSH) protocol covering transport layer, user authentication, and connection multiplexing.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: None detected
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Keywords: required by, shall use
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: Regions: United States, International, Bodies: NIST
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Tatu Ylonen; Chris M. Lonvick; Tero Kivinen; Timo J. Rinne; Sami Lehtinen; Markku-Juhani O. Saarinen; Darren Moffat
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: SSH; TCP/IP; X11; telnet; rlogin
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Host Keys; Certification Authority (CA); Local database for host keys
- **Standardization Bodies**: IETF; Internet Society; Network Working Group; secsh WG
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: FIPS-186-2; FIPS-180-2; RFC2119; RFC2434; STD 1
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: DSS; SHA-1
- **Key Takeaways**: SSH architecture consists of three major components: Transport Layer, User Authentication Protocol, and Connection Protocol; Host key verification can use local databases or trusted certification authorities to prevent man-in-the-middle attacks; Implementations should provide options to verify host keys via fingerprints derived from SHA-1 hashes; The protocol allows for local extensions through a specific algorithm naming system using DNS names; Security properties include server authentication, confidentiality, integrity, and optional perfect forward secrecy.

## RFC 4302

- **Reference ID**: RFC 4302
- **Title**: IKEv2: Internet Key Exchange Protocol Version 2
- **Authors**: IETF
- **Publication Date**: 2005-12-01
- **Last Updated**: 2005-12-01
- **Document Status**: Proposed Standard
- **Main Topic**: Specification of the Internet Key Exchange version 2 (IKEv2) protocol for mutual authentication and establishing security associations within IPsec.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: None detected
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: C. Kaufman, Ed.
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: IKEv2, IPsec, ESP, AH, ISAKMP, DOI, NAT Traversal, EAP, IPComp
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Key Management, Security Associations
- **Standardization Bodies**: IETF, Internet Society, IANA
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: Diffie-Hellman, PKIX Certificates, pre-shared keys, ENCR_NULL, NONE
- **Key Takeaways**: IKEv2 does not interoperate with version 1 but shares enough header format to run over the same UDP port; Implementations must not negotiate NONE as the IKE integrity protection algorithm or ENCR_NULL as the IKE encryption algorithm; Pre-shared keys must contain as much randomness as the strongest key being negotiated to avoid dictionary and social engineering attacks; NAT detection hashes may allow attackers to discover internal IP addresses by brute-forcing the 32-bit IPv4 address space; Long IKEv2 messages requiring IP fragmentation are vulnerable to buffer exhaustion attacks by attackers.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: None detected
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Support**: None detected

- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Covered**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Evidence**: None detected
- **Lifecycle State**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: None detected
- **Target Audience**: Developer, Security Architect
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Support for canonical network byte order; compliance with Security Architecture document; support for ICMP PMTU messages or equivalent internal signaling
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: vpn-ssh-pqc, crypto-agility
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: Denial of Service via half-open connections; NAT address discovery via hash brute-forcing; Buffer exhaustion via IP fragmentation
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: None detected
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: Conformance testing against specification requirements; interoperability via IKE signaling
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: None detected

- **Authors**: IETF

- **Last Updated**: 2006-03-01

- **Reference ID**: RFC 4718

- **Authors**: IETF

- **Last Updated**: 2006-10-01

- **Last Updated**: 2007-11-01

## RFC 5869

- **Reference ID**: RFC 5869
- **Title**: HMAC-based Extract-and-Expand Key Derivation Function (HKDF)
- **Authors**: IETF; H. Krawczyk; P. Eronen
- **Publication Date**: 2010-05-01
- **Last Updated**: 2010-05-01
- **Document Status**: Informational
- **Main Topic**: Specification of a simple HMAC-based Extract-and-Expand Key Derivation Function (HKDF) for deriving cryptographically strong secret keys from input keying material.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: None detected
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: H. Krawczyk; P. Eronen
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: IKEv2; PANA; EAP-AKA; TLS; IKEv1
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Key Management
- **Standardization Bodies**: Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF); IETF Trust; Internet Engineering Steering Group (IESG)
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: HMAC; Diffie-Hellman; RSA
- **Key Takeaways**: HKDF follows an extract-then-expand paradigm to concentrate dispersed entropy into a pseudorandom key before expansion; The use of salt significantly strengthens HKDF by ensuring independence between different uses of the hash function; The 'info' input is critical for binding derived keys to specific contexts to prevent cross-protocol key reuse; The extract step should not be skipped when input keying material is a Diffie-Hellman value as it is not uniformly random; Applications may skip the extract step only if the input keying material is already a cryptographically strong pseudorandom string.

- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected

- **Authors**: IETF
- **Publication Date**: 2010-08-01
- **Last Updated**: 2010-08-01
- **Document Status**: Proposed Standard

- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA; DSA; ECC
- **Key Takeaways**: OneAsymmetricKey syntax accommodates both private and public key components for better flexibility; CMS content types like SignedData and EncryptedData can protect asymmetric key packages; .p8 files may be PEM-encoded with specific headers for EncryptedPrivateKeyInfo or PrivateKeyInfo; Disclosure of private-key material can lead to masquerades, requiring strong encryption algorithms for protection; The document obsoletes RFC 5208 to rename PrivateKeyInfo to OneAsymmetricKey.

## RFC 5990

- **Reference ID**: RFC 5990
- **Title**: Use of the RSA-KEM Key Transport Algorithm in CMS
- **Authors**: IETF
- **Publication Date**: 2010-09-01
- **Last Updated**: 2010-09-01
- **Document Status**: Proposed Standard
- **Main Topic**: Specifies the conventions for using the RSA-KEM Key Transport Algorithm with the Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS).
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: None detected
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: J. Randall, B. Kaliski, J. Brainard, S. Turner (authors); Russ Housley (guidance and encouragement); Blake Ramsdell, Jim Schaad (helpful direction); Magnus Nystrom (assistance on Appendix B); Bob Griffin, John Linn (editorial direction and procedural guidance)
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: CMS, S/MIME
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Key Management
- **Standardization Bodies**: IETF, ASC X9, ISO/IEC JTC1 SC27, NIST
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: NIST Cryptographic Module Validation Program (CMVP)
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA, SHA-1, Camellia, Triple-DES, AES, RSAES-OAEP, PKCS #1 v1.5
- **Key Takeaways**: RSA-KEM provides higher security assurance than PKCS #1 v1.5 by encrypting a random integer and using symmetric key-wrapping; RSA key pairs used for RSA-KEM should not be employed for data encryption or authentication to maintain provable security; Implementations must support specific underlying components including KDF based on SHA-1 and key wrap algorithms like AES or Triple-DES; The algorithm is recommended as a replacement for vulnerable RSA encryption schemes in new CMS-based applications.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: 128-bit security level; 128, 192, or 256-bit key sizes for AES/Camellia; 112 or 168-bit key sizes for Triple-DES; 128-bit data block size for AES/Camellia; 64-bit data block size for Triple-DES
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Support**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Pure PQC Signature Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Support**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Covered**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Evidence**: None detected
- **Lifecycle State**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: None detected
- **Target Audience**: Developer, Security Architect
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Support for KDF based on SHA-1; Support for AES Key Wrap or Triple-DES Key Wrap; Support for Camellia Key Wrap if Camellia is supported; Secure deletion of intermediate results; Protection of RSA private key and content-encryption key
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Algorithms, crypto-agility, email-signing, pki-workshop
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: Chosen-ciphertext attacks; Timing attacks (IntegerToString conversion time consistency); Compromise of RSA private key; Compromise of content-encryption key
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: None detected

## RFC 6176

- **Reference ID**: RFC 6176
- **Title**: Prohibiting Secure Sockets Layer (SSL) Version 2.0
- **Authors**: IETF
- **Publication Date**: 2011-03-01
- **Last Updated**: 2011-03-01
- **Document Status**: Proposed Standard
- **Main Topic**: This document formally prohibits the negotiation of Secure Sockets Layer (SSL) version 2.0 in Transport Layer Security (TLS) connections due to known security deficiencies.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: None detected
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Sean Turner and Tim Polk are the document authors; Peter Saint Andre and Simon Josefsson inspired the idea; Michael D'Errico, Paul Hoffman, Nikos Mavrogiannopoulos, Tom Petch, Yngve Pettersen, Marsh Ray, Martin Rex, Yaron Sheffer, and Glen Zorn provided reviews and comments.
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: TLS, SSL 2.0, SSL 3.0, TLS 1.0, TLS 1.1, TLS 1.2
- **Infrastructure Layers**: None detected
- **Standardization Bodies**: Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF), Internet Engineering Steering Group (IESG)
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: MD5
- **Key Takeaways**: TLS clients MUST NOT send SSL version 2.0 compatible CLIENT-HELLO messages; TLS servers MUST NOT reply with an SSL 2.0 SERVER-HELLO with a protocol version less than { 0x03, 0x00 }; SSL 2.0 is prohibited due to deficiencies including unprotected handshake messages and weak message authentication using MD5; The prohibition updates backward compatibility sections in TLS standards to phase out legacy protocol support.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: None detected
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Support**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Pure PQC Signature Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Support**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Covered**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Evidence**: None detected
- **Lifecycle State**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: None detected
- **Target Audience**: Developer, Security Architect
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: None detected
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: tls-basics, crypto-agility
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: Man-in-the-middle attacks exploiting unprotected handshake messages; Session termination via TCP FIN insertion; Weak cipher suite selection due to lack of handshake protection.
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: None detected
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: None detected
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: None detected
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: None detected
- **Constrained Device & IoT Suitability**: None detected
- **Supply Chain & Vendor Risk**: None detected
- **Deployment & Migration Complexity**: Backward compatibility sections in TLS are updated to prohibit SSL 2.0 negotiation; Servers may continue to accept version 2 CLIENT-HELLO format but must not negotiate SSL 2.0; The number of servers supporting the "MAY accept" option is declining.
- **Financial & Business Impact**: None detected
- **Organizational Readiness**: None detected

- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: SHA-256
- **Key Takeaways**: Certificate Transparency logs provide public auditability to detect misissued TLS certificates; Merkle Hash Trees ensure the append-only property of logs and allow efficient consistency proofs; Clients can require Signed Certificate Timestamps in TLS handshakes to verify certificate logging; Monitors can regularly query logs to check for unexpected certificate issuance for specific domains; Logs do not prevent misissue but enable detection and evidence collection.

## RFC 7250

- **Reference ID**: RFC 7250
- **Title**: Using Raw Public Keys in Transport Layer Security (TLS) and DTLS
- **Authors**: IETF TLS
- **Publication Date**: 2014-06-01
- **Last Updated**: 2014-06-01
- **Document Status**: Proposed Standard
- **Main Topic**: Specification of a new certificate type and TLS extensions to enable the use of raw public keys in TLS and DTLS handshakes, eliminating X.509 certificate chain overhead.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: None detected
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: Regions: International
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Paul Wouters; Hannes Tschofenig; John Gilmore; Sam Weiler; Tero Kivinen
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: TLS; DTLS; DANE; LDAP; CoAP; PKIX
- **Infrastructure Layers**: PKI; Firmware provisioning
- **Standardization Bodies**: IETF; IESG
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA; DSA; ECDSA
- **Key Takeaways**: Raw public keys reduce certificate overhead by approximately 70% for ultra-constrained IoT devices; Implementation requires only a minimalistic ASN.1 parser instead of full PKIX validation code; Authentication is only valid when an out-of-band mechanism binds the public key to the entity; The specification reuses the SubjectPublicKeyInfo structure from PKIX certificates; Raw public keys can be combined with TLS Cached Info extension to exchange just fingerprints.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: None detected
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Combined usage of raw public keys and X.509 certificates
- **Pure PQC KEM Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Support**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Pure PQC Signature Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Support**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Covered**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Evidence**: None detected
- **Lifecycle State**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: ~70% size reduction compared to original certificate; minimalistic ASN.1 parser required
- **Target Audience**: Developer; Security Architect; IoT Engineer
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Minimalistic ASN.1 parser; out-of-band mechanism for key binding; support for TLS extensions client_certificate_type and server_certificate_type

- **Key Takeaways**: JWTs encode claims as a JSON object used as payload for JWS or plaintext for JWE; Claims can be digitally signed, integrity protected with MAC, or encrypted; Registered claim names include iss, sub, aud, exp, iat, nbf, and jti; Nested JWTs allow signing and/or encryption to be employed within an enclosing structure; Unsecured JWTs exist where claims are not integrity protected or encrypted.

## RFC 7568

- **Reference ID**: RFC 7568
- **Title**: Deprecating Secure Sockets Layer Version 3.0
- **Authors**: IETF
- **Publication Date**: 2015-06-01
- **Last Updated**: 2015-06-01
- **Document Status**: Proposed Standard
- **Main Topic**: Formal deprecation of Secure Sockets Layer version 3.0 (SSLv3) due to comprehensive security flaws, requiring its prohibition in favor of more secure TLS versions.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: None detected
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: R. Barnes, M. Thomson, A. Pironti, A. Langley
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: SSLv3, TLS 1.0, TLS 1.1, TLS 1.2, DTLS
- **Infrastructure Layers**: None detected
- **Standardization Bodies**: Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF), Internet Engineering Steering Group (IESG)
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RC4, SHA-1, MD5, SHA-256, ECDH, ECDSA
- **Key Takeaways**: SSLv3 MUST NOT be used and negotiation of SSLv3 from any version of TLS MUST NOT be permitted; SSLv3 is comprehensively broken due to flawed CBC padding, weak stream ciphers, and vulnerable key exchange mechanisms; TLS servers MUST accept any record layer version number for ClientHello but MUST NOT negotiate SSLv3; SSLv3 lacks support for modern security features such as AEAD modes, ECDH, and stateless session tickets; Custom cryptographic primitives in SSLv3 rely on weak hash algorithms like SHA-1 and MD5.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: None detected
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Support**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Pure PQC Signature Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Support**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Covered**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Evidence**: None detected
- **Lifecycle State**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: None detected
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Developer, Compliance Officer
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: None detected
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: tls-basics, crypto-agility, migration-program
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: POODLE attack, man-in-the-middle attacks during renegotiation or session resumption, non-deterministic padding in CBC construction, biases in RC4 stream cipher
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: None detected
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: None detected
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: None detected
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: None detected
- **Constrained Device & IoT Suitability**: None detected
- **Supply Chain & Vendor Risk**: None detected
- **Deployment & Migration Complexity**: Backward compatibility issues noted as TLS implementations historically permitted SSLv3 negotiation; prohibition of fallback to SSLv3 required.
- **Financial & Business Impact**: None detected

## RFC 7748

- **Reference ID**: RFC 7748
- **Title**: Elliptic Curves for Security (X25519, X448)
- **Authors**: IETF
- **Publication Date**: 2016-01-01
- **Last Updated**: 2016-01-01
- **Document Status**: Informational
- **Main Topic**: Specification of Curve25519 and Curve448 elliptic curves and their associated X25519 and X448 Diffie-Hellman functions for cryptographic key agreement.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: None detected
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Large quantum computers breaking elliptic curves
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: A. Langley, M. Hamburg, S. Turner
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: Transport Layer Security (TLS), Diffie-Hellman
- **Infrastructure Layers**: None detected
- **Standardization Bodies**: Internet Research Task Force (IRTF), Crypto Forum Research Group, IETF Trust
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: Diffie-Hellman, X25519, X448, Curve25519, Curve448
- **Key Takeaways**: Curve25519 and Curve448 are generated deterministically based on required properties; Curve25519 operates at ~128-bit security level while Curve448 operates at ~224-bit security level; Implementors must address side-channel attacks such as variable timing in machine instructions; Protocol designers must not assume contributory behavior due to cofactors of 8 and 4; Curve448 is provided to hedge against analytical advances against elliptic curves for designs with relaxed performance requirements
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ~128-bit security level, ~224-bit security level
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected

- **Authors**: IETF

## RFC 8268

- **Reference ID**: RFC 8268
- **Title**: More Modular Exponentiation (MODP) Diffie-Hellman Groups for SSH
- **Authors**: IETF
- **Publication Date**: 2017-12-01
- **Last Updated**: 2017-12-01
- **Document Status**: Proposed Standard
- **Main Topic**: This document defines additional Modular Exponentiation (MODP) Diffie-Hellman key exchange groups for the Secure Shell (SSH) protocol using SHA-2 hashes to address security concerns with smaller groups and SHA-1.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: None detected
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: M. Baushke
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: Secure Shell (SSH)
- **Infrastructure Layers**: None detected
- **Standardization Bodies**: Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF); Internet Engineering Steering Group (IESG); IANA
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: NIST SP 800-131Ar1; NIAP standards
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: Diffie-Hellman (DH); Modular Exponentiation (MODP); SHA-1; SHA-2; SHA-256; SHA-512; RSA; Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman (ECDH); ECDSA; NIST P-384
- **Key Takeaways**: Implementers should request support for larger DH MODP group sizes (4096-8192 bits) to mitigate security concerns; The move to the SHA-2 family of hashes is required for data-integrity verification in key exchange; Fixed Diffie-Hellman parameters are high-value targets for pre-computation, so generating additional primes or using larger values is recommended; RSA moduli should have a minimum size of 3072 bits; Elliptic Curves are restricted to NIST P-384 for ECDH and ECDSA in accordance with NIST and NIAP standards
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: 110 bits of security; 310 bits of security; 2048 bits; 3072 bits; 4096-8192 bit groups
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Support**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Pure PQC Signature Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Support**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Covered**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Evidence**: None detected
- **Lifecycle State**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: None detected
- **Target Audience**: Developer; Security Architect
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Support for SHA-2 family of secure hash algorithms; Sufficient entropy and/or deterministic random-bit generator (DRBG) algorithms
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: vpn-ssh-pqc; crypto-agility; compliance-strategy
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: Pre-computation attacks on fixed Diffie-Hellman parameters; Insufficient entropy
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: None detected
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: None detected
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: None detected
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: Deterministic random-bit generator (DRBG); Sufficient entropy

## RFC 8702

- **Reference ID**: RFC 8702
- **Title**: Use of the SHAKE One-Way Hash Functions in the Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS)
- **Authors**: IETF
- **Publication Date**: 2020-01-01
- **Last Updated**: 2020-01-01
- **Document Status**: Proposed Standard
- **Main Topic**: Specifies conventions for using SHAKE128 and SHAKE256 hash functions in Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) with RSASSA-PSS and ECDSA.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: None detected
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected

- **Infrastructure Layers**: PKI, Public Key Infrastructure Using X.509 (PKIX)
- **Standardization Bodies**: Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF), NIST, IANA
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: FIPS 202, SP800-78-4, SP800-107, SP800-185, BCP 78
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA, RSASSA-PSS, ECDSA, SHAKE128, SHAKE256, SHA3-224, SHA3-256, SHA3-384, SHA3-512, SHA256, SHA512, KMAC
- **Key Takeaways**: SHAKE128 and SHAKE256 are defined as new hash functions for use in CMS; SHAKE128 with 32-byte output offers 128 bits of collision and preimage resistance; SHAKE128 OIDs are recommended with 2048-bit RSA for 112-bit security; Conforming implementations must use specific OIDs for RSASSA-PSS and ECDSA with SHAKEs; Identifier parameters for these algorithms must be absent in CMS messages
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: 128 bits of collision and preimage resistance; 32 bytes output length; 64 bytes output length; 2048-bit RSA; 112-bit security

## RFC 8725

- **Reference ID**: RFC 8725
- **Title**: JSON Web Token Best Current Practices
- **Authors**: IETF
- **Publication Date**: 2020-02-01
- **Last Updated**: 2020-02-01
- **Document Status**: Best Current Practice
- **Main Topic**: This document provides best current practices and security guidance for the implementation and deployment of JSON Web Tokens (JWTs) to mitigate known vulnerabilities.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: None detected
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Years mentioned: 2020; Keywords: deprecat
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: Regions: United States, International, Bodies: NIST
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Yaron Sheffer; Dick Hardt; Michael B. Jones
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: TLS; OAuth 2.0; OpenID Connect; LDAP; HTTPS
- **Infrastructure Layers**: API Security, Identity & Access Management
- **Standardization Bodies**: IETF; IESG
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: BCP 78; BCP 14; RFC 2119; RFC 8174; RFC 7519; RFC 7515; RFC 7516; RFC 7518; RFC 7841; RFC 8017; RFC 7159; RFC 8259
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA; HMAC-SHA256; SHA-256; ECDH-ES; RSA-PKCS1 v1.5; RSAES
- **Key Takeaways**: Libraries must enable callers to specify a supported set of algorithms and reject any others; Applications must validate that the algorithm header matches the cryptographic operation performed; The "none" algorithm should only be used when JWTs are protected by other means such as TLS; Implementers must avoid weak symmetric keys with insufficient entropy; Recipients must validate issuer, subject, and audience claims to prevent substitution attacks.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: 2048-bit RSA
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Cryptographic agility
- **Pure PQC KEM Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Support**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Pure PQC Signature Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Support**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Covered**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Evidence**: None detected
- **Lifecycle State**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: None detected
- **Target Audience**: Developer; Security Architect; Researcher
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Support for UTF-8 encoding; Validation of cryptographic inputs; Sufficient entropy for cryptographic keys; Avoidance of compression on encryption inputs
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: api-security-jwt, crypto-agility, digital-id, tls-basics
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: nonce reuse in ECDSA signatures; weak symmetric keys with insufficient entropy; algorithm confusion attacks (RS256 to HS256 substitution); none-algorithm vulnerabilities; compression oracle attacks via ciphertext length analysis; invalid curve point injection in ECDH-ES; JSON encoding ambiguity bypasses; server-side request forgery (SSRF) and injection attacks via JWT claims
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: algorithm enumeration via "alg" Header Parameter validation; deprecated cipher detection for RSA-PKCS1 v1.5; cryptographic agility requirements to manage algorithm deprecation
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: conformance testing against RFC 7519, RFC 7515, RFC 7516, and RFC 7518; validation of all cryptographic operations including nested JWTs; explicit typing validation; audience and issuer claim validation
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: None detected
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: requirement for unique random values in ECDSA signatures; recommendation to use deterministic approach (RFC 6979) to counter predictable random value attacks; requirement for cryptographic keys to have sufficient entropy
- **Constrained Device & IoT Suitability**: None detected
- **Supply Chain & Vendor Risk**: third-party library trust risks regarding under-specified security mechanisms and incomplete implementations; open-source vs proprietary considerations via IETF Trust Legal Provisions and Simplified BSD License requirements
- **Deployment & Migration Complexity**: cryptographic agility design requirement to handle algorithm deprecation; phased rollout considerations for stricter requirements in specific specifications; backward compatibility challenges with older JSON encodings (UTF-16, UTF-32) vs current standard (UTF-8); migration from RSA-PKCS1 v1.5 to RSAES-OAEP
- **Financial & Business Impact**: None detected
- **Organizational Readiness**: governance prerequisites for implementers of JWT libraries and code using such libraries; change management scope for developers of specifications relying on JWTs; maturity assessment via adherence to Best Current Practice (BCP) minimum recommendations
- **Math Family**: None detected
- **PQC Round**: None detected

## RFC 8784

- **Reference ID**: RFC 8784
- **Title**: Mixing Preshared Keys in IKEv2 for Post-quantum Security
- **Authors**: IETF IPSECME
- **Publication Date**: 2020-06-01
- **Last Updated**: 2020-06-01
- **Document Status**: Standards Track
- **Main Topic**: RFC 8784 defines an extension to IKEv2 that mixes Post-quantum Preshared Keys (PPKs) into key derivation to provide quantum resistance without replacing existing authentication methods.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: None detected
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Harvest Now Decrypt Later; Shor's Algorithm (implied by breaking DH/ECDH)
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: S. Fluhrer; P. Kampanakis; D. McGrew; V. Smyslov
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: IKEv2; IPsec
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Key Management
- **Standardization Bodies**: IETF
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: Diffie-Hellman; Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman; Pseudorandom Function
- **Key Takeaways**: PPKs provide quantum resistance by mixing into SK_d, SK_pi, and SK_pr during initial IKE SA setup; Existing IKEv2 authentication and key exchange mechanisms remain in place and are strengthened rather than replaced; PPK usage can be configured as mandatory or optional per peer; The PPK must not be used during IKE SA rekey, resumption, or similar operations; Implementations must handle PPK_ID mismatches and optional PPK fallback via NO_PPK_AUTH notifications
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: 256 bits of entropy; 128 bits of post-quantum security; Category 5 equivalent
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Mixing Preshared Keys; Strengthening existing authentication with additional secret
- **Pure PQC KEM Support**: No
- **Pure PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Support**: No
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Pure PQC Signature Support**: No
- **Pure PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Support**: No
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Covered**: IKE/IPsec
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Evidence**: IKE/IPsec: "extension of IKEv2 to allow it to be resistant to a quantum computer by using preshared keys"
- **Lifecycle State**: Released
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: None detected
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect; Developer; Operations
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: List of Post-quantum Preshared Keys (PPKs) and identifiers; Configurable flag for mandatory PPK usage; Support for USE_PPK and PPK_IDENTITY notifications
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: vpn-ssh-pqc; hybrid-crypto; crypto-agility; pqc-risk-management
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: Downgrade attacks by removing USE_PPK notification; dictionary search over plausible PPK values; impersonation attacks
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: Audit events where PPK was configured but not used
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: None detected
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: None detected
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: 256 bits of entropy requirement for PPK
- **Constrained Device & IoT Suitability**: None detected
- **Supply Chain & Vendor Risk**: None detected
- **Deployment & Migration Complexity**: Backward compatibility maintained; Optional PPK support allows gradual rollout; Mandatory PPK requires coordinated configuration
- **Financial & Business Impact**: None detected
- **Organizational Readiness**: None detected
- **Math Family**: None detected
- **PQC Round**: None detected

- **Key Takeaways**: Certificate compression reduces TLS handshake data by approximately 30% to mitigate bloat on constrained IoT devices; The mechanism is only supported in TLS 1.3 and newer due to middlebox compatibility issues with unencrypted certificate messages; Implementations must bound memory usage and limit decompressed chain size to prevent resource exhaustion attacks; Compression alters message lengths, requiring padding schemes to address potential side-channel inference from wire traffic.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: None detected
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Support**: None detected

- **Pure PQC Signature Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Support**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Covered**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Evidence**: None detected
- **Lifecycle State**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Reduces certificate chain overhead by ~30%; TLS framing imposes a 16777216-byte limit on certificate message size; Supports zlib, brotli, and zstd compression algorithms.
- **Target Audience**: Developer; Security Architect; Operations
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: TLS 1.3 or newer; Support for zlib, brotli, or zstd compression libraries; Memory usage bounds for decompression; Uncompressed length validation logic.
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: tls-basics; pki-workshop; iot-ot-pqc

## RFC 9001

- **Reference ID**: RFC 9001
- **Title**: Using TLS to Secure QUIC
- **Authors**: IETF
- **Publication Date**: 2021-05-01
- **Last Updated**: 2021-05-01
- **Document Status**: Proposed Standard
- **Main Topic**: Defines how Transport Layer Security (TLS) 1.3 is used to secure the QUIC transport protocol, including handshake integration, packet protection, and key management.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: None detected
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: M. Thomson (Editor); S. Turner (Editor)
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: TLS 1.3; QUIC
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Key Management
- **Standardization Bodies**: Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF); Internet Engineering Steering Group (IESG)
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: AEAD_AES_128_GCM; AEAD_AES_128_CCM; AEAD_AES_256_GCM; AEAD_CHACHA20_POLY1305; SHA-256; HKDF; HMAC
- **Key Takeaways**: QUIC integrates TLS 1.3 for connection establishment and record protection, requiring specific handling of encryption levels and key discarding; Header protection algorithms must be defined for negotiated cipher suites, with specific schemes provided for AES and ChaCha20 AEADs; Endpoints must manage timing side channels during key updates by pre-generating receive keys or using randomized keys to prevent leakage of Key Phase bits; Initial packets lack secret key protection, making them vulnerable to tampering, though some modifications are detectable via TLS message integrity; Replay attacks via 0-RTT data are a recognized risk that requires implementation of TLS 1.3 replay protections.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: None detected
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Support**: None detected

- **Pure PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Support**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Covered**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Evidence**: None detected
- **Lifecycle State**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: 16-byte authentication tag; 16 bytes larger output than input for defined cipher suites; 128-bit authentication tag size; 128-bit block function size; 128-bit key size for AES-128 variants; 256-bit key size for AES-256 variant
- **Target Audience**: Developer; Security Architect
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Support for TLS 1.3; Implementation of header protection schemes for negotiated cipher suites; Ability to handle multiple encryption levels simultaneously; Compliance with BCP 14 terminology
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: tls-basics; crypto-agility; migration-program
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: Timing side channels; Replay attacks; Packet tampering; Header protection bypass
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: None detected
