- **Authors**: IETF OAuth Working Group
- **Publication Date**: 2025-06-01
- **Last Updated**: 2025-06-01
- **Document Status**: Published RFC
- **Main Topic**: RFC 9901 defines a mechanism for selective disclosure of JSON Web Token claims using salted hashes and optional key binding to enhance privacy in credential presentation.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: None detected

- **Standardization Bodies**: Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF)
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: RFC 9901; BCP 78
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: None detected
- **Key Takeaways**: SD-JWT enables selective disclosure of claims by replacing cleartext data with salted digests in the signed payload; Key Binding allows holders to prove possession of a private key via a separate Key Binding JWT; The format supports both compact and JSON serialization for transporting issuer-signed data and disclosures; Privacy is enhanced through unlinkability and decoy digests to prevent guessing of non-disclosed values.

## RFC-9909

- **Reference ID**: RFC-9909
- **Title**: Profile for Use of SLH-DSA in the Internet X.509 PKI
- **Authors**: IETF LAMPS; Jake Massimo (AWS); Scott Fluhrer (Cisco)
- **Publication Date**: 2025-10-01
- **Last Updated**: 2025-10-01
- **Document Status**: Proposed Standard
- **Main Topic**: Defines X.509 algorithm identifiers and parameter conventions for all 12 SLH-DSA parameter sets, including absent-parameters rules and self-signed certificate requirements.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: SLH-DSA
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Post-Quantum
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Years mentioned: 2021, 2025
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: United States; NIST; IETF
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: K. Bashiri; S. Fluhrer; S. Gazdag; D. Van Geest; S. Kousidis
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: X.509
- **Infrastructure Layers**: PKI
- **Standardization Bodies**: IETF; NIST
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: FIPS 205; FIPS 180; FIPS 202
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: SHA-2; SHAKE256; SHA-256; SHA-512; SHAKE128
- **Key Takeaways**: SLH-DSA OIDs are defined for 12 parameter sets covering pure and pre-hash modes; The parameters component in AlgorithmIdentifier MUST be absent for SLH-DSA; Raw SLH-DSA signatures are encoded directly in BIT STRING without ASN.1 wrapping; Context string for SLH-DSA in X.509 is restricted to the empty string; Both Pure SLH-DSA and HashSLH-DSA are specified for use in PKIX certificates and CRLs
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: 128-bit security level; 192-bit security level; 256-bit security level; SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s; SLH-DSA-SHA2-128f; SLH-DSA-SHA2-192s; SLH-DSA-SHA2-192f; SLH-DSA-SHA2-256s; SLH-DSA-SHA2-256f; SLH-DSA-SHAKE-128s; SLH-DSA-SHAKE-128f; SLH-DSA-SHAKE-192s; SLH-DSA-SHAKE-192f; SLH-DSA-SHAKE-256s; SLH-DSA-SHAKE-256f
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Support**: Not Applicable
- **Pure PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Support**: Not Applicable
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Pure PQC Signature Support**: Yes
- **Pure PQC Signature Evidence**: "This document specifies the use of both Pure SLH-DSA and HashSLH-DSA in Public Key Infrastructure X.509"
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Support**: No
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Covered**: X.509

## Radboud-MTC-Thesis-2025

- **Reference ID**: Radboud-MTC-Thesis-2025
- **Title**: Implementation and Analysis of Merkle Tree Certificates for Post-Quantum Secure Authentication in TLS
- **Authors**: M. Pohl (Radboud University)
- **Publication Date**: 2025-06-01
- **Last Updated**: 2025-06-01
- **Document Status**: Published
- **Main Topic**: Implementation and performance analysis of Merkle Tree Certificates as an optimization for post-quantum secure authentication in TLS 1.3.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-DSA
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Harvest now, decrypt later attack
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Years mentioned: 2020, 2021, 2024, 2025
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: Regions: United States, Germany, International, Bodies: NIST

- **Protocols Covered**: TLS 1.3; X.509; OCSP; ACME; Certificate Transparency; KEMTLS; HTTPS; IMAP; SMTP; LDAP

- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: None detected

- **Title**: The Learning with Errors Problem
- **Authors**: Oded Regev (Courant Institute, NYU)
- **Publication Date**: 2010-01-01
- **Last Updated**: 2010-01-01
- **Document Status**: Published
- **Main Topic**: A foundational survey describing the Learning with Errors (LWE) problem, its hardness based on worst-case lattice problems, and its cryptographic applications.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: LWE, Ring-LWE, SIS, LPN
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Quantum Computer, Post-Quantum
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected

- **Standardization Bodies**: IEEE
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: Gaussian elimination
- **Key Takeaways**: LWE hardness is based on worst-case lattice problems like GAPSVP and SIVP; The best known algorithms for LWE run in exponential time even with quantum computers; Ring-LWE reduces key sizes from quadratic to almost linear size using structure and fast Fourier transform; LWE supports diverse cryptographic constructions including public-key encryption, identity-based encryption, and oblivious transfer

- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Evidence**: None detected

- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Key sizes typically on the order of n squared for SIS and LWE; Ring-LWE reduces key size to almost linear O(n); Operations sped up using fast Fourier transform

## Rosenpass-Protocol

- **Reference ID**: Rosenpass-Protocol
- **Title**: Rosenpass: Formally Verified Post-Quantum Protocol for WireGuard
- **Authors**: Rosenpass e.V.
- **Publication Date**: 2023-02-01
- **Last Updated**: 2024-06-01
- **Document Status**: White Paper
- **Main Topic**: Rosenpass is a formally verified post-quantum authenticated key exchange protocol designed to secure WireGuard VPNs using a hybrid of Classic McEliece and Kyber.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: Classic McEliece, Kyber
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Post-Quantum
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Years mentioned: 2025; Keywords: deprecat
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: Regions: United States, Bodies: NIST
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Karolin Varner; Benjamin Lipp; Wanja Zaeske; Lisa Schmidt; Prabhpreet Dua
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: Rosenpass; liboqs; libsodium
- **Protocols Covered**: WireGuard; Noise IK; NTP
- **Infrastructure Layers**: VPN, Key Management
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: BLAKE2b; SHAKE256; ChaCha20Poly1305; XChaCha20Poly1305; AES
- **Key Takeaways**: Rosenpass generates a post-quantum secure shared key every two minutes to inject as a WireGuard pre-shared key; The protocol uses a biscuit cookie mechanism to mitigate state disruption attacks by storing responder state in an encrypted cookie; Security relies on Classic McEliece for authenticity and Kyber for forward secrecy; Implementations must use different rekey intervals for initiator (130s) and responder (120s) roles to manage handshake turns.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: Classic McEliece 4608961 claims 192-bit AES security; Kyber-512 claims 128-bit AES security; Symmetric keys are 32 bytes long
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid Rosenpass/WireGuard setup combining post-quantum key exchange with WireGuard pre-shared key injection

- **Hybrid PQC KEM Support**: None detected

- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Classic McEliece public keys 524160 bytes; Classic McEliece ciphertexts 188 bytes; Classic McEliece secret keys 13568 bytes; Kyber public keys 800 bytes; Kyber ciphertexts 768 bytes; Kyber secret keys 1632 bytes
- **Target Audience**: Engineer; Researcher; Developer
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Rust implementation; libsodium library for hash, AEAD, and XAEAD; liboqs library for post-quantum KEMs; proper zeroization of temporary variables
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: vpn-ssh-pqc; hybrid-crypto; algorithms; tls-basics

- **Key Takeaways**: The specification extends BIP-0032 to support non-secp256k1 curves including ed25519 and curve25519; Public key derivation is not supported for ed25519 and curve25519, requiring hardened derivation only; Invalid keys are handled by retrying the hash operation rather than leaving keys undefined; Different salts are used for different curves to prevent private key reuse across elliptic curves with different orders.

- **Key Takeaways**: SPHINCS+ is a stateless hash-based signature scheme relying solely on hash function security; The scheme uses a hypertree structure to authenticate few-time signature key pairs without maintaining state; Parameter sets are available for SHA-2 and SHAKE instantiations at security levels 1, 3, and 5; The design includes multi-target attack protection via keyed hash functions and tweakable hash functions; SPHINCS+ offers 'simple' and 'robust' instantiations trading off speed for security argument strength

- **Security Levels & Parameters**: MODERATE level targeting 128-bit security; ADVANCED level targeting 256-bit security; RSA at least 3072 bits; Diffie-Hellman at least 3072 bits; AES 128-bit, 192-bit, 256-bit; SNOW 2.0 128-bit, 256-bit; IV at least 128 bits

## Signal-PQXDH-Spec

- **Reference ID**: Signal-PQXDH-Spec
- **Title**: PQXDH: The Post-Quantum Extended Diffie-Hellman Protocol
- **Authors**: Signal Messenger
- **Publication Date**: 2023-09-19
- **Last Updated**: 2024-12-01
- **Document Status**: Published
- **Main Topic**: Specification of the PQXDH key agreement protocol extending X3DH with post-quantum security using ML-KEM for Signal Messenger.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: Crystals-Kyber-1024, CRYSTALS-KYBER-1024
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Passive quantum adversaries; Active quantum adversaries
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Years mentioned: 2023, 2024
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Ehren Kret; Rolfe Schmidt
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: Signal Messenger
- **Protocols Covered**: PQXDH; X3DH; XEdDSA; HKDF; AEAD
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Key Management; Server trust
- **Standardization Bodies**: None detected
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: curve25519; curve448; SHA-256; SHA-512; X25519; X448; HKDF
- **Key Takeaways**: PQXDH provides post-quantum forward secrecy while relying on discrete log hardness for authentication; Implementations must support both one-time and last-resort post-quantum prekeys to handle server storage limits; The protocol requires pairwise disjoint encoding ranges for elliptic curve and KEM keys; Alice must verify signatures on all prekeys before generating ephemeral keys; Hybrid key establishment combines classical Diffie-Hellman outputs with post-quantum encapsulated shared secrets.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: IND-CCA post-quantum security; IND-CPA and INT-CTXT post-quantum security; 256 or 512-bit hash function; 32 bytes HKDF output; 64-byte random values for signatures
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid key agreement combining Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman and Post-Quantum Key Encapsulation Mechanism; Dual use of classical identity keys and post-quantum prekeys
- **Pure PQC KEM Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected

- **Target Audience**: Developer; Security Architect; Researcher
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Support for XEdDSA signatures; Implementation of EncodeEC and DecodeEC functions with single-byte curve constants; Implementation of EncodeKEM and DecodeKEM functions with single-byte pqkem constants; Ability to manage one-time prekey bundles on a server

## Solana-PQC-Helius

- **Reference ID**: Solana-PQC-Helius
- **Title**: What Would Solana Need to Change to Become Quantum Ready?
- **Authors**: Solana
- **Publication Date**: Not specified
- **Last Updated**: Not specified
- **Document Status**: Published
- **Main Topic**: Helius Docs Sign In Open main menu Products Solutions Resources Blog Pricing Stake 6.35 % Docs Sign In Blog / Research What Would Solana Need to Change to Become Quantum Ready?
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-DSA, SLH-DSA, FN-DSA, Falcon, SPHINCS+, CRYSTALS-Dilithium
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Quantum Computer, Post-Quantum
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Years mentioned: 2025, 2026
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: Regions: United States, Bodies: NIST
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Adam Pospisil (author); Kobi, Lostin, Quentin, Ichigo, Aseneca, Dean (reviewers)
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: Solana
- **Protocols Covered**: Alpenglow, Rotor, Turbine
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Blockchain
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: Ed25519, RSA, Diffie–Hellman, SHA-256, BLS
- **Key Takeaways**: Solana addresses are public keys by default, leaving all externally owned accounts vulnerable to Shor's algorithm without a hidden layer; PQ migration requires new address formats using hashes of PQ public keys and signature scheme identifiers; Votor consensus lacks efficient PQ signature aggregation alternatives like BLS, necessitating bandwidth adjustments or validator set reduction; Rotor data propagation cannot embed large PQ signatures in every shred, requiring Merkle root signing per slice or block-level signing instead.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: 128 bits security for Ed25519, ML-DSA, FN-DSA, SLH-DSA; Ed25519 public key 32 B; Ed25519 signature 64 B; ML-DSA public key 1312 B; ML-DSA signature 2560 B; FN-DSA public key 897 B; FN-DSA signature 666 B; SLH-DSA public key 64 B; SLH-DSA signature 7856 B
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Vault constructions based on hash-based one-time signature schemes; coexistence of PQ addresses with existing Ed25519 addresses during migration; authenticated channels between validators
- **Pure PQC KEM Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Support**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Pure PQC Signature Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Support**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Covered**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Evidence**: None detected
- **Lifecycle State**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Solana transaction-size limit increasing to 4096 bytes in 2026; ML-DSA public key 1312 B; ML-DSA signature 2560 B; FN-DSA public key 897 B; FN-DSA signature 666 B; SLH-DSA signature 7856 B
- **Target Audience**: Developer, Security Architect, Researcher
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: New address format and transaction version; hash of transaction payload for transaction ID; Merkle root computation over shreds; authenticated channels between validators
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Threats, Algorithms, Migration-program, digital-assets, merkle-tree-certs
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: None detected

- **Reference ID**: Springer-MathFoundations-PQC-2026
- **Title**: Mathematical Foundations for Post-Quantum Cryptography
- **Authors**: Tsuyoshi Takagi; Masato Wakayama; Noboru Kunihiro; Keisuke Tanaka; Kazufumi Kimoto; Momonari Kudo (Eds.) — University of Tokyo; NTT Inc.; University of Tsukuba; Institute of Science Tokyo; University of the Ryukyus; Fukuoka Institute of Technology
- **Publication Date**: 2026-01-01
- **Last Updated**: 2026-01-01
- **Document Status**: Published
- **Main Topic**: This book presents mathematical foundations for post-quantum cryptography, covering lattice theory, code-based schemes, hash-based signatures, isogeny-based cryptography, and multivariate schemes as part of the Crypto-Math CREST project.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: LWE, MLWE, NTRU, Module-LWE, ROLLO, RQC, UOV, Middle-Product Learning with Errors, (U,U+V)-Code Problem

- **Infrastructure Layers**: blockchains for large-scale distributed systems

- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA, ECDLP

- **Reference ID**: Sui-PQC-Blog
- **Title**: Securing Sui in the Quantum Computing Era
- **Authors**: Sui
- **Publication Date**: Not specified
- **Last Updated**: Not specified
- **Document Status**: Published
- **Main Topic**: Sui.io Home About Contribute an Article Cryptography Securing Sui in the Quantum Computing Era Although quantum computing threatens the cryptographic security of blockchains, builders can take measures now to secure their Sui apps.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM, ML-DSA, Falcon, SPHINCS+, CRYSTALS-Kyber, CRYSTALS-Dilithium
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Shor's Algorithm, Quantum Computer, Post-Quantum
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Milestones: ST) has mandated the transition to quantum-resistant cryptographic standards by 2035, deprecating classical algorithm... | -resistant cryptographic standards by 2035, deprecating classical algorithms by 2030
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: Regions: United States, Bodies: NIST
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: None detected
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: OpenSSL, Signal, oqs-provider, Solana, Algorand, Cardano

- **Infrastructure Layers**: Blockchain
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA, ECC, EdDSA, ECDSA, BLS12-381, AES, ElGamal, SHA-256 (implied by 256-bit security context), Pinocchio, Groth16
- **Key Takeaways**: Sui must transition to lattice-based schemes like ML-KEM and ML-DSA to mitigate Shor's algorithm threats; High-value data requires immediate PQ encryption to prevent store now decrypt later attacks; Hash functions need increased output sizes (256-bit to 384-bit) to maintain security against Grover's algorithm; Lattice-based signatures offer better batch verification potential than hash-based schemes like SPHINCS+ for high-throughput blockchains
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: 128 bits, 192 bits, 256 bits, ML-KEM 800 B public key, ML-KEM 768 B ciphertext, ML-DSA 1312 B public key, ML-DSA 2420 B signature
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: PreQ-PQ 1-out-of-2 multisig with expiration time, proactive strategies at account creation, adaptive strategies for existing addresses, PQ ZK proof of seed randomness

## TCG-TPM-2.0-Library-v1.85-Errata

- **Reference ID**: TCG-TPM-2.0-Library-v1.85-Errata
- **Title**: TCG TPM 2.0 Library Specification v1.85 — Errata
- **Authors**: Trusted Computing Group (TCG)
- **Publication Date**: 2026-05-15
- **Last Updated**: 2026-05-15
- **Document Status**: Released (Errata)
- **Main Topic**: Errata and clarifications for the TCG Trusted Platform Module Library Specification v1.85, specifically addressing ML-DSA, ML-KEM, and Labeled KEM command definitions.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-DSA; ML-KEM
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: None detected
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: TPM
- **Infrastructure Layers**: HSM
- **Standardization Bodies**: Trusted Computing Group; National Institute of Standards and Technology
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: FIPS 204; FIPS 186-5
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA; ECC; EdDSA; MAC
- **Key Takeaways**: TPM implementations should interpret TPM_RC_NO_RESULT as the expected error for ML-DSA rejection sampling limits; For signatures without a scheme hash like ML-DSA, TPM2_Quote and TPM2_NV_Certify should use the Name algorithm of the signing key for digest computation; TPMs may return TPM_RC_SIZE for large NV index certifications with ML-DSA to avoid excessively long responses; TPM2_VerifyDigestSignature should return a NULL ticket for external Mu verification with ML-DSA to prevent nonviable tickets
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: None detected
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected

## Testing.Measuring.and.Managing.PQC.Migration.WP

- **Reference ID**: Testing.Measuring.and.Managing.PQC.Migration.WP
- **Title**: Testing.Measuring.and.Managing.PQC.Migration.WP
- **Authors**: Testing.Measuring.and.Managing.PQC.Migration.WP.pdf
- **Publication Date**: Not specified
- **Last Updated**: Not specified
- **Document Status**: Published
- **Main Topic**: Real-world scale testing reveals the significant performance degradation and infrastructure costs of migrating to Post-Quantum Cryptography, emphasizing the need for early measurement and planning.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM-768, Kyber
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: None detected
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: VIAVI TeraVM Security Test, HAProxy, Strongswan, OpenSSL
- **Protocols Covered**: TLS, IKEv2, IPsec, QUIC
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Next Generation Firewalls (NGFWs), secure web gateways (SGWs), proxy chains, hardware security modules (HSMs), FPGA-based accelerators
- **Standardization Bodies**: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: FIPS-203
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: X25519, Diffie-Hellman, RSA, ECC
- **Key Takeaways**: Realistic scale testing is required to understand the true performance cost of PQC, as specification documents do not reflect real-world infrastructure strain; Hybrid cryptography (PQC + classical) should be used to reduce overhead while maintaining security; Hardware acceleration via CPUs with vector instructions or specialized accelerators is necessary because existing RSA/ECC chips cannot accelerate PQC algorithms; TLS session resumption can eliminate 80-90% of PQC performance impact for returning users by reducing handshake overhead.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ML-KEM-768
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid X25519Kyber768 mechanism, Hybrid Mode (PQC + classical)
- **Pure PQC KEM Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Support**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Pure PQC Signature Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Support**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Covered**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Evidence**: None detected
- **Lifecycle State**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: TLS connection rate reduced from 51,420 to 32,320 per second; Latency increased from 2-3 ms to almost 100 ms; Time to first byte jumped from 2 ms to more than 80 ms; Web page loading time 48x slower; VPN tunnel establishment rate fell from over 17,000 to under 5,000 per second; Decryption capacity cut by 50–90% on existing hardware; Hardware insufficiency margins of 50 to 75 percent; Session resumption eliminates 80-90% of PQC performance impact.
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, CISO, Operations
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Hardware sized according to classical cryptographic assumptions becomes insufficient; Existing security appliances depend on hardware designed for RSA and ECC acceleration which cannot accelerate Kyber/ML-KEM; NGFWs often assume fixed sizes for TLS parsing which PQ-TLS exceeds.
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Assess, Migrate, hybrid-crypto, vpn-ssh-pqc, pqc-risk-management, pqc-business-case
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: None detected
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: None detected
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: production-scale benchmarking; real-world scale testing; stress testing; controlled testing using HAProxy with OpenSSL and StrongSwan; measuring latency, connection rates, tunnel setup times, throughput, timeout behavior and failure modes
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: Quantum Key Distribution (QKD)
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: None detected
- **Constrained Device & IoT Suitability**: None detected
- **Supply Chain & Vendor Risk**: Vendors acknowledge many deployments will require new hardware; Existing hardware sized for classical crypto becomes insufficient.
- **Deployment & Migration Complexity**: hybrid X25519Kyber768 mechanism; hybrid cryptography (PQC + classical); TLS session resumption; hardware sizing adjustments; infrastructure upgrades; phased planning (measure, size, adjust, redesign, optimize); backward compatibility challenges with NGFWs and fixed-size assumptions
- **Financial & Business Impact**: saving millions later; cost of emergency fixes; hardware replacement costs; performance degradation costs
- **Organizational Readiness**: proper planning; measurement and benchmarking; systematic optimization; early testing; careful planning; tuning systems
- **Math Family**: None detected
- **PQC Round**: None detected

## UK NCSC PQC Guidance

- **Reference ID**: UK NCSC PQC Guidance
- **Title**: Preparing for Quantum-Safe Cryptography
- **Authors**: UK NCSC
- **Publication Date**: 2020-11-11
- **Last Updated**: 2020-11-11
- **Document Status**: White Paper
- **Main Topic**: UK NCSC position paper on quantum computing threat to public-key cryptography; recommends QSC following NIST standardisation; rejects QKD for government/military use.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: XMSS, LMS
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: CRQC (Cryptographically Relevant Quantum Computer), Cryptographically Relevant Quantum, Quantum Computer, Post-Quantum
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Years mentioned: 2020, 2022
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: Regions: United States, International, Bodies: NIST, NCSC
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: UK NCSC cryptography team
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: TLS, IPsec
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Firmware, Key Management
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST, ETSI, NCSC
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: NCSC Guidance
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: AES
- **Key Takeaways**: Adopt quantum-safe cryptography following NIST standardisation rather than early non-standardised adoption; reject Quantum Key Distribution for government and military applications; factor quantum threats into long-term roadmaps for systems with high-value data or long certificate lifetimes; maintain support for conventional PKC during the interim transition period
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: None detected
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Operating both conventional and quantum-safe cryptography to ease transition; pre-placed symmetric keys in addition to key agreement with conventional PKC
- **Pure PQC KEM Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected

- **Target Audience**: Technical policymakers, Large organisations, Public sector, Cyber security professionals
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Conduct investigatory work to identify high priority systems; plan for long-term investment decisions; wait for standards-compliant QSC products
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Threats, Timeline, Migrate, qkd, stateful-signatures
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: None detected
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: identify which of their systems will be high priority for transition; parts of the public-key infrastructure that have certificate expiry dates far into the future
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: None detected
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: Quantum Key Distribution (QKD); NCSC does not endorse QKD for any government or military applications
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: Quantum Random Number Generation mentioned in NCSC White Paper on Quantum Security Technologies
- **Constrained Device & IoT Suitability**: deployment in constrained devices
- **Supply Chain & Vendor Risk**: risks to business continuity if there are unforeseen dependencies on cryptographic components that require replacement; third-party library trust (implied by caution against non-standardised products and interoperability risks)
- **Deployment & Migration Complexity**: operate both conventional and quantum-safe cryptography; continue support for conventional PKC for the interim period; transition is an inherently complex and expensive process; risk in beginning transition to QSC before standards-compliant products are available
- **Financial & Business Impact**: inherently complex and expensive process; unnecessary expenditure in having to transition a second time to standards-compliant QSC
- **Organizational Readiness**: factor the threat of quantum computer attacks into their long-term roadmaps; conduct investigatory work; long-term investment decisions; robust and secure transition will take time to plan and deliver

- **Key Takeaways**: System owners should begin financial planning for PQC updates during technology refreshes; Use hybrid PQ/T schemes as interim measures for interoperability and implementation security; Prefer single migration to fully post-quantum PKI over intermediate hybrid PKI due to complexity; Operational systems should only use PQC based on robust implementations of final NIST standards, not draft standards; ML-KEM-768 and ML-DSA-65 are recommended for most general-purpose use cases

## USENIX-2024-HQC-Division-Timing

- **Reference ID**: USENIX-2024-HQC-Division-Timing
- **Title**: Divide and Surrender: Exploiting Variable Division Instruction Timing in HQC Key Recovery Attacks
- **Authors**: Schroeder et al.; USENIX Security
- **Publication Date**: 2024-08-01
- **Last Updated**: 2024-08-01
- **Document Status**: Published
- **Main Topic**: The paper demonstrates a timing side-channel attack named Divide and Surrender that exploits variable-time division instructions in HQC implementations to recover secret keys.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: HQC, KyberSlash
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Robin Leander Schröder; Stefan Gast; Qian Guo
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: None detected
- **Infrastructure Layers**: None detected
- **Standardization Bodies**: None detected
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: None detected
- **Key Takeaways**: Compilers may emit variable-time division instructions when modulo operations are used in loops, creating timing side-channels; A DIV-SMT approach enables precise measurement of small division timing variations on processors supporting Simultaneous Multithreading; An attacker can build a Plaintext-Checking oracle with above 90% accuracy using only 100 side-channel traces; The Zero Tester method allows recovery of HQC secret keys in under 2 minutes on an AMD Zen2 machine; Exploitation requires co-location of the attacker on the same physical core as the victim.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: HQC-128
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Support**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Pure PQC Signature Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Support**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Covered**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Evidence**: None detected
- **Lifecycle State**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: 100 side-channel traces required for oracle construction; under 2 minutes attack completion time on AMD Zen2 machine; 8-fold decrease in idealized oracle queries compared to previous approaches
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Developer, Researcher
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Processors supporting Simultaneous Multithreading (SMT); co-location of attacker and victim on the same physical core
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Algorithms; Threats; Assess
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: timing side-channel (variable division instruction timing, DIV-SMT)

## VIAVI-PQC-Migration-WP-2026

- **Reference ID**: VIAVI-PQC-Migration-WP-2026
- **Title**: Testing, Measuring and Managing PQC Migration: The Hidden Cost of Quantum-Safe Security and How to Plan PQC Migration
- **Authors**: VIAVI Solutions
- **Publication Date**: 2026-01-01
- **Last Updated**: 2026-01-01
- **Document Status**: Published
- **Main Topic**: Real-world scale performance benchmarking of PQC migration using VIAVI TeraVM Security Test on Dell R6625 hardware.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Post-Quantum
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Years mentioned: 2026
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: Regions: United States, Bodies: NIST
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: None detected
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: OpenSSL, strongSwan, HAProxy, VIAVI TeraVM Security Test
- **Protocols Covered**: TLS, IPsec, IKEv2, HTTPS, QUIC
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Cloud, VPN
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: FIPS-203
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: X25519, Diffie-Hellman, RSA, ECC
- **Key Takeaways**: PQC migration causes significant performance slowdowns in connection rates and latency that cannot be predicted by specifications alone; Enterprise security appliances lacking PQC hardware acceleration may lose 50–90% of decryption capacity; TLS session resumption can eliminate 80-90% of PQC performance impact for returning users; Organizations must benchmark infrastructure under realistic conditions before deploying PQC to avoid silent failures and timeout issues.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ML-KEM-768, X25519Kyber768
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid Mode (PQC + classical), hybrid X25519Kyber768 mechanism, TLS session resumption
- **Pure PQC KEM Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Support**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Pure PQC Signature Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Support**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Covered**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Evidence**: None detected
- **Lifecycle State**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: HTTPS connections per second reduced from 51,420 to 32,320; Latency increased from 2-3ms to almost 100ms; Time to first byte jumped from 2ms to over 80ms; Web page loading time 48x slower; VPN tunnel establishment rate fell from over 17,000 to under 5,000 per second; Connection storms stretched from six seconds to twenty; Decryption capacity cut by 50–90% on non-accelerated hardware.
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, CISO, Operations, Developer
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: CPUs with vector instructions (AVX2/AVX-512); GPUs for parallelizable operations; Hardware security modules (HSMs) or FPGA-based accelerators optimized for PQC; TeraVM Security Test software; Dell R6625 hardware; OpenSSL (PQC-enabled)
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Assess, Migrate, Algorithms, hybrid-crypto, tls-basics, vpn-ssh-pqc
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: None detected
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: None detected
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: Real-world scale performance benchmarking; production-scale benchmarking using TeraVM Security Test software; stress testing under realistic conditions; measuring latency, connection rates, tunnel setup times, throughput, timeout behavior and failure modes
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) based secure communication
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: None detected
- **Constrained Device & IoT Suitability**: None detected
- **Supply Chain & Vendor Risk**: None detected
- **Deployment & Migration Complexity**: Migration phase (assess/plan/test/migrate/launch); backward compatibility approaches (Hybrid Mode: Use hybrid cryptography (PQC + classical)); breaking changes (32% throughput drop, 3500%+ latency increase, 75% reduction in VPN tunnel setup rate); infrastructure sizing adjustments (hardware sized according to classical cryptographic assumptions becomes insufficient); phased rollout implications (work that determines whether that migration is smooth or painful must begin now)
- **Financial & Business Impact**: Cost of emergency fixes; spending more; save millions later; PQC is mandatory, but it is not free
- **Organizational Readiness**: Proper planning; systematic optimization; test early, plan carefully, and tune their systems with eyes open; gathering hard data; measuring performance metrics before deployment
- **Math Family**: None detected
- **PQC Round**: None detected

## draft-bonnell-lamps-chameleon-certs-07

- **Reference ID**: draft-bonnell-lamps-chameleon-certs-07
- **Title**: A Mechanism for Encoding Differences in Paired Certificates (Chameleon Certs)
- **Authors**: IETF LAMPS; Corey Bonnell (DigiCert); John Gray (Entrust); D. Hook (KeyFactor); Tomofumi Okubo (DigiCert); Mike Ounsworth (Entrust)
- **Publication Date**: 2023-05-31
- **Last Updated**: 2025-10-18
- **Document Status**: Active Internet-Draft
- **Main Topic**: Defines an X.509v3 extension mechanism to encode differences between paired certificates, allowing reconstruction of both traditional and PQC certificates from a single certificate.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-DSA-65
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Years mentioned: 2025, 2026
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: Regions: International
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Corey Bonnell, John Gray, D. Hook, Tomofumi Okubo, Mike Ounsworth
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: X.509, PKCS #10
- **Infrastructure Layers**: PKI
- **Standardization Bodies**: IETF
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: BCP 78, BCP 79, RFC 5280, RFC 2119, RFC 8174
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: EC P-521
- **Key Takeaways**: The mechanism encodes only differences between paired certificates to achieve space savings over full certificate encoding; Relying parties can reconstruct both traditional and PQC certificates from a single Base Certificate without changing path validation algorithms; The approach supports algorithm migration by allowing subjects to hold multiple keys of different types in a single certificate structure; Backward compatibility is maintained by selecting which certificate (Base or Delta) to send based on recipient capabilities.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ML-DSA-65, EC P-521
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Paired certificates, encoding differences between certificates, algorithm migration, backward compatibility via certificate selection
- **Pure PQC KEM Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Support**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Pure PQC Signature Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Support**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Covered**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Evidence**: None detected
- **Lifecycle State**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Space savings realized by encoding only differences between paired certificates rather than the entire paired certificate
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Developer, PKI Administrator
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Support for X.509 v3 extensions; Ability to process PKCS #10 Certificate Signing Request attributes; Capability to reconstruct Delta Certificates from Base Certificates
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: pki-workshop, migration-program, hybrid-crypto, crypto-agility, algorithms
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: None detected
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: certificate inventory, algorithm enumeration

## draft-celi-wiggers-tls-authkem

- **Reference ID**: draft-celi-wiggers-tls-authkem
- **Title**: TLS AuthKEM — Certificate-Based Authentication with KEM in TLS 1.3
- **Authors**: IETF
- **Publication Date**: 2023-10-01
- **Last Updated**: 2023-10-01
- **Document Status**: Internet Draft
- **Main Topic**: Proposes a Key Encapsulation Mechanism (KEM)-based authentication mechanism for TLS 1.3 that replaces certificate-based signature authentication with KEM-based authentication using ML-KEM.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM, ML-DSA, Falcon
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: T. Wiggers (PQShield); S. Celi (Brave Software); P. Schwabe (Radboud University and MPI-SP); D. Stebila (University of Waterloo); N. Sullivan
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: TLS 1.3, X.509, HPKE, OCSP, Certificate Transparency
- **Infrastructure Layers**: PKI, Key Management
- **Standardization Bodies**: IETF, NIST
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA-2048, Ed25519, Ed448, X25519
- **Key Takeaways**: AuthKEM replaces TLS 1.3 signature-based authentication with KEM-based authentication to eliminate classical signature algorithms from the handshake; Using ML-KEM-768 reduces handshake authentication data exchange compared to Dilithium-2 signatures; The proposal introduces a new KEMEncapsulation handshake message and modifies the key schedule to mix in shared secrets from authentication; Certificates must contain long-term KEM public keys and be handled in accordance with RFC 8446; Side effects may include an adversary learning that a client has a certificate by observing message lengths.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ML-KEM-768, ML-KEM-512, Dilithium-2, Falcon-512, RSA-2048
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Support**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Pure PQC Signature Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Support**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Covered**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Evidence**: None detected
- **Lifecycle State**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: TLS 1.3 RSA-2048 handshake auth 528 bytes; TLS 1.3 RSA-2048 certificate chain 784 bytes; TLS 1.3 Dilithium-2 handshake auth 3732 bytes; TLS 1.3 Dilithium-2 certificate chain 6152 bytes; TLS 1.3 Falcon-512 handshake auth 1563 bytes; TLS 1.3 Falcon-512 certificate chain 2229 bytes; AuthKEM Kyber-768 handshake auth 2272 bytes; AuthKEM Kyber-768 with Dilithium-2 certificate chain 6152 bytes; AuthKEM Kyber-768 with Falcon-512 certificate chain 2229 bytes
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Developer, Researcher
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: None detected
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Algorithms, tls-basics, pki-workshop, migration-program
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: Side effects where adversary learns client has a certificate by observing message lengths; Side effects when client is prompted to approve use or unlock a certificate stored encrypted or on a smart card

## draft-celi-wiggers-tls-authkem-07

- **Reference ID**: draft-celi-wiggers-tls-authkem-07
- **Title**: KEM-based Authentication for TLS 1.3 (AuthKEM)
- **Authors**: T. Wiggers; S. Celi; P. Schwabe; D. Stebila; N. Sullivan
- **Publication Date**: 2021-07-01
- **Last Updated**: 2026-05-04
- **Document Status**: Draft (Individual Submission, Informational)
- **Main Topic**: This document proposes a Key Encapsulation Mechanism (KEM)-based authentication mechanism for TLS 1.3 to replace classical signature-based authentication.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM, ML-DSA, Falcon
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: T. Wiggers (PQShield); S. Celi (Brave Software); P. Schwabe (Radboud University and MPI-SP); D. Stebila (University of Waterloo); N. Sullivan

- **Protocols Covered**: TLS 1.3, X.509
- **Infrastructure Layers**: PKI
- **Standardization Bodies**: IETF, NIST
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA, ECDSA, DH
- **Key Takeaways**: AuthKEM reduces handshake authentication data size by replacing signatures with KEM operations; KEM-based authentication allows reusing ephemeral key exchange implementation for authentication, reducing code size; Falcon-512 is suitable for offline certificate chain signatures but risky for online handshake signatures due to side-channel vulnerabilities; Combining AuthKEM with certificate compression mechanisms yields significant handshake size reductions in WebPKI settings.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ML-KEM-768, ML-KEM-512, Dilithium-2, Falcon-512, RSA-2048

- **Target Audience**: Security Architect; Developer; Researcher

## draft-fregly-dnsop-slh-dsa-mtl-dnssec-06

- **Reference ID**: draft-fregly-dnsop-slh-dsa-mtl-dnssec-06
- **Title**: SLH-DSA in Merkle Tree Ladder Mode for DNSSEC
- **Authors**: A. Fregly; J. Harvey; B. Kaliski; D. Wessels (Verisign Labs)
- **Publication Date**: 2024-04-01
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-30
- **Document Status**: Draft (Individual Submission)
- **Main Topic**: Specifies the application of SLH-DSA in Merkle Tree Ladder mode to DNSSEC to mitigate large signature sizes.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: SLH-DSA
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Quantum computers that can break current signature algorithms
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: A.M. Fregly; J. Harvey; B. Kaliski; D. Wessels
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: DNSSEC
- **Infrastructure Layers**: HSM
- **Standardization Bodies**: IETF; NIST
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: FIPS 205
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA; ECDSA; SHA2; SHAKE
- **Key Takeaways**: MTL mode reduces PQC signature size impact by using condensed signatures (Merkle proofs) primarily; SLH-DSA is selected for DNSSEC due to Merkle tree basis and large signature size benefits; Batch signing RRSets reduces computational overhead and HSM load; Resolvers should query without mtl-mode-full first, then re-issue if full signatures are needed; Large responses with mtl-mode-full should use TCP, TLS, or QUIC to avoid truncation
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s; SLH-DSA-SHAKE-128s
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Support**: Not Applicable
- **Pure PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Support**: Not Applicable
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Pure PQC Signature Support**: Yes
- **Pure PQC Signature Evidence**: "SLH-DSA-MTL Signature scheme" for DNSSEC
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Support**: No
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Covered**: DNSSEC
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Evidence**: DNSSEC: "apply the Stateless Hash-Based Digital Signature Algorithm in Merkle Tree Ladder mode to the DNS Security Extensions"
- **Lifecycle State**: Draft
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: 32-octet key size; variable-length signature; reduced memory requirements for zone databases; reduced average computational overhead per message signed
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect; Developer; Operations
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: EDNS(0) support; IANA algorithm number registration; MTL mode-aware client implementation
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Algorithms; merkle-tree-certs; dnssec-pqc

## draft-ietf-hpke-pq

- **Reference ID**: draft-ietf-hpke-pq
- **Title**: Post-Quantum and Post-Quantum/Traditional Hybrid Algorithms for HPKE
- **Authors**: IETF HPKE WG
- **Publication Date**: 2024-03-01
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-01
- **Document Status**: Internet-Draft
- **Main Topic**: This document defines post-quantum and hybrid post-quantum/traditional Key Encapsulation Mechanisms for HPKE to enable quantum-safe encryption.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM-512, ML-KEM-768, ML-KEM-1024
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: harvest now, decrypt later attacks; inference of decapsulation keys from encapsulation keys by a cryptographically relevant quantum computer
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Years mentioned: 2026
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: Regions: United States, International, Bodies: NIST
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Richard Barnes; Deirdre Connolly
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: HPKE, Messaging Layer Security (MLS), Oblivious HTTP, TLS Encrypted ClientHello extension
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Key Encapsulation Mechanism (KEM); Key Derivation Function (KDF); Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data (AEAD)
- **Standardization Bodies**: Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF); NIST
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: BCP 78; BCP 79; FIPS203; FIPS186; RFC7748; RFC9180; RFC9420; RFC9458
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: X25519, P-256, P-384, X448, AES-128-GCM, AES-256-GCM, ChaCha20Poly1305, SHAKE256, HKDF-SHA256, HKDF-SHA384, HKDF-SHA512
- **Key Takeaways**: Hybrid constructions combining PQ and traditional KEMs provide security fallback if the PQ algorithm fails; HPKE can be made resilient to quantum computer attacks by integrating ML-KEM; SHA-3 based KDFs are defined for compatibility with ML-KEM internal operations; The document supports both pure PQ and PQ/T hybrid modes for interoperability.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: NIST category 3; NIST category 5; ML-KEM-512; ML-KEM-768; ML-KEM-1024
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid constructions of post-quantum KEMs with traditional KEMs; X25519 + ML-KEM-768; P-256 + ML-KEM-768; P-384 + ML-KEM-1024

- **Pure PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected

- **Implementation Prerequisites**: SHAKE256 KDF implementation; ML-KEM.KeyGen_internal function from FIPS203; pseudorandom bytes meeting randomness requirements of FIPS203

- **Reference ID**: draft-ietf-ipsecme-hybrid-kem-ikev2-frodo-00
- **Title**: Post-quantum Hybrid Key Exchange in IKEv2 with FrodoKEM
- **Authors**: C. Wang et al. (IETF IPSECME WG)
- **Publication Date**: 2026-03-11
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-11
- **Document Status**: Draft (WG Document)
- **Main Topic**: Specifies the use of FrodoKEM as an additional or standalone key exchange mechanism in IKEv2 for post-quantum hybrid key exchange.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: FrodoKEM, ML-KEM
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Cryptographically Relevant Quantum Computer (CRQC); harvest-now-and-decrypt-later (HNDL) attack
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected

- **Standardization Bodies**: IETF; ISO
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: AES; SHAKE; Diffie-Hellman; ECDH
- **Key Takeaways**: FrodoKEM can be used as an additional key exchange or standalone in IKEv2; FrodoKEM key sizes are roughly 13 times larger than ML-KEM, likely triggering IP fragmentation; The document specifies FrodoKEM variants for NIST security levels 3 and 5 only; Recipient tests are not required for FrodoKEM public keys or ciphertexts; Both AES and SHAKE variants are included to optimize performance across different hardware platforms.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: NIST security levels 3 and 5; FrodoKEM-976; FrodoKEM-1344; FrodoKEM-976-AES; FrodoKEM-976-SHAKE; FrodoKEM-1344-AES; FrodoKEM-1344-SHAKE

- **Pure PQC KEM Evidence**: "Clarifications are added that FrodoKEM can be used either as an additional key exchange method, or as the only key exchange method"
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Support**: Yes
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Evidence**: "This draft specifies how to use FrodoKEM by itself or as an additional key exchange in IKEv2 along with a traditional key exchange"
- **Pure PQC Signature Support**: No
- **Pure PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected

- **Hybrid PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected

- **Performance & Size Considerations**: FrodoKEM-976 decapsulation key 31,296 bytes; FrodoKEM-976 encapsulation key 15,632 bytes; FrodoKEM-976 ciphertext 15,792 bytes; FrodoKEM-1344 decapsulation key 43,088 bytes; FrodoKEM-1344 encapsulation key 21,520 bytes; FrodoKEM-1344 ciphertext 21,696 bytes; FrodoKEM sizes roughly 13 times larger than ML-KEM

## draft-ietf-ipsecme-ikev2-downgrade-prevention

- **Reference ID**: draft-ietf-ipsecme-ikev2-downgrade-prevention
- **Title**: Downgrade Prevention for the Internet Key Exchange Protocol Version 2 (IKEv2)
- **Authors**: IETF IPSECME WG
- **Publication Date**: 2024-01-01
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-01
- **Document Status**: Internet-Draft
- **Main Topic**: Specifies an extension to IKEv2 to prevent algorithm downgrade attacks by having peers confirm participation in the same conversation.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: None detected
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Quantum computer breaking traditional key exchange methods
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Years mentioned: 2026
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: Regions: International
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Valery Smyslov; Christopher Patton
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: IKEv2; IPsec
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Key Management
- **Standardization Bodies**: IETF
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: BCP 78; BCP 79; Revised BSD License
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: SIGMA
- **Key Takeaways**: IKEv2 authentication allows downgrade attacks where an attacker forces peers to use weak key exchange methods; The proposed extension prevents this by having peers confirm they participated in the same conversation; The attack is relevant during migration to post-quantum algorithms due to coexistence of old and new algorithms; An attacker with a long-term authentication key can modify AUTH payloads to authenticate modified IKE_SA_INIT requests
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: None detected
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: hybrid PQ/T key exchange
- **Pure PQC KEM Support**: No
- **Pure PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Support**: No
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected

- **Hybrid PQC Signature Support**: No
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Covered**: IKE/IPsec
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Evidence**: IKE/IPsec: "extension to the Internet Key Exchange protocol version 2 (IKEv2)"
- **Lifecycle State**: Draft
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: None detected
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect; Developer
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Familiarity with IKEv2 protocol [RFC7296]; Implementation of modified authentication data construction in IKEv2 peers
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: crypto-agility; vpn-ssh-pqc; pqc-risk-management
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: key-compromise impersonation (KCI) attack; identity misbinding attack
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: None detected

## draft-ietf-ipsecme-ikev2-mlkem

- **Reference ID**: draft-ietf-ipsecme-ikev2-mlkem
- **Title**: Post-quantum Hybrid Key Exchange with ML-KEM in IKEv2
- **Authors**: IETF IPSECME WG
- **Publication Date**: 2023-11-01
- **Last Updated**: 2025-09-29
- **Document Status**: Internet-Draft
- **Main Topic**: Specification of ML-KEM integration into IKEv2 for quantum-resistant key establishment in VPN and IPsec tunnels.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM, ML-KEM-512, ML-KEM-768, ML-KEM-1024
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Cryptographically Relevant Quantum Computer (CRQC); harvest-now-decrypt-later attack
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Years mentioned: 2024, 2025, 2026

- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: (EC)DH, AES-128, AES-192, AES-256
- **Key Takeaways**: ML-KEM can be used alone or combined with traditional key exchanges in IKEv2; Hybrid PQ/T key exchanges prevent fragmentation issues caused by large ML-KEM parameters; ML-KEM-768 and ML-KEM-1024 may exceed network MTU requiring multiple packets; Implementations must perform input validation checks per FIPS 203 before encapsulation or decapsulation; Three new algorithm identifiers (35, 36, 37) are registered for IKEv2.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: NIST Level 1, NIST Level 3, NIST Level 5, ML-KEM-512, ML-KEM-768, ML-KEM-1024, 32-byte shared secret
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Post-Quantum Traditional (PQ/T) Hybrid key exchange; combining traditional (EC)DH with ML-KEM

## draft-ietf-ipsecme-ikev2-mlkem-03

- **Reference ID**: draft-ietf-ipsecme-ikev2-mlkem-03
- **Title**: Post-quantum Hybrid Key Exchange with ML-KEM in IKEv2
- **Authors**: IETF IPSECME WG
- **Publication Date**: 2023-11-01
- **Last Updated**: 2025-09-29
- **Document Status**: Internet-Draft
- **Main Topic**: Specifies the integration of ML-KEM as a quantum-resistant key exchange mechanism in IKEv2, supporting both standalone and hybrid deployments with traditional algorithms.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Cryptographically relevant quantum computers (CRQC)
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Panos Kampanakis (Author)
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: IKEv2, IPsec
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Key Management
- **Standardization Bodies**: IETF, NIST
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: FIPS 203
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: AES-128, AES-192, AES-256, (EC)DH
- **Key Takeaways**: Use ML-KEM in IKEv2 via IKE_INTERMEDIATE or IKE_FOLLOWUP_KE messages to handle large packet sizes that exceed network MTUs; Implement PQ/T Hybrid key exchanges to combine traditional algorithm security with quantum-resistant protection against CRQCs; Disable support for traditional-only key exchange sequences to prevent active downgrade attacks by on-path attackers; ML-KEM-512 and ML-KEM-768 may fit within standard IKE_SA_INIT messages, while ML-KEM-1024 typically requires fragmentation or additional exchanges.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: NIST Level 1, NIST Level 3, NIST Level 5, ML-KEM-512, ML-KEM-768, ML-KEM-1024
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Post-Quantum Traditional (PQ/T) Hybrid key exchange
- **Pure PQC KEM Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Support**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Evidence**: None detected
- **Pure PQC Signature Support**: None detected
- **Pure PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Support**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Covered**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Evidence**: None detected
- **Lifecycle State**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: ML-KEM-768 and ML-KEM-1024 public key and ciphertext sizes can exceed network MTU; ML-KEM-512 and ML-KEM-768 may not push UDP packet size larger than typical network MTUs; No noticeable performance impact on long-lived tunnels
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Developer, Network Engineer
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Support for IKE_INTERMEDIATE or IKE_FOLLOWUP_KE messages; IKEv2 Message Fragmentation support (RFC7383); Multiple Key Exchanges in IKEv2 support (RFC9370)
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: hybrid-crypto, vpn-ssh-pqc, crypto-agility, migration-program
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: Downgrade attacks forcing negotiation of weakest key exchange method; Active man-in-the-middle attacks
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: None detected
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: None detected
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: None detected
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: None detected

## draft-ietf-ipsecme-ikev2-mlkem-05

- **Reference ID**: draft-ietf-ipsecme-ikev2-mlkem-05
- **Title**: ML-KEM Key Exchange in IKEv2
- **Authors**: P. Kampanakis (IETF IPSECME WG)
- **Publication Date**: 2024-12-01
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-14
- **Document Status**: Draft (Publication Requested)
- **Main Topic**: Specifies the integration of ML-KEM as a standalone or hybrid key exchange mechanism within the IKEv2 protocol to provide quantum-resistant key establishment.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM, ML-KEM-512, ML-KEM-768, ML-KEM-1024
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Cryptographically Relevant Quantum Computer (CRQC); harvest-now-decrypt-later attack
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: P. Kampanakis
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: IKEv2; IPsec
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Key Management
- **Standardization Bodies**: IETF; NIST
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: Diffie-Hellman; Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman; AES-128; AES-192; AES-256
- **Key Takeaways**: ML-KEM can be used as a standalone key exchange in IKEv2 IKE_SA_INIT only for ML-KEM-512 due to MTU constraints; Hybrid PQ/T key exchanges are recommended to prevent fragmentation and combine security properties; Implementations must perform strict input validation on ML-KEM public keys and ciphertexts per FIPS 203; Downgrade attacks remain a risk if traditional-only key exchange sequences are supported; Randomness used for ML-KEM must be independent of other IKEv2 negotiation seeds.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: NIST Level 1; NIST Level 3; NIST Level 5; ML-KEM-512; ML-KEM-768; ML-KEM-1024
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Post-Quantum Traditional (PQ/T) Hybrid key exchange; combining ML-KEM with traditional (EC)DH
- **Pure PQC KEM Support**: Yes
- **Pure PQC KEM Evidence**: "ML-KEM-512 ... MAY be used in IKE_SA_INIT as a quantum-resistant-only key exchange"
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Support**: Yes
- **Hybrid PQC KEM Evidence**: "combined with a traditional (EC)DH key exchange in IKEv2"
- **Pure PQC Signature Support**: No
- **Pure PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Support**: No
- **Hybrid PQC Signature Evidence**: None detected
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Covered**: IKE/IPsec
- **PQC Heatmap Protocols Evidence**: IKE/IPsec: "Post-quantum Key Exchange with ML-KEM in the Internet Key Exchange Protocol Version 2 (IKEv2)"
- **Lifecycle State**: Draft
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: ML-KEM-512 Payload Length 808/776; ML-KEM-512 Data Size 800/768 octets; ML-KEM-768 Payload Length 1192/1096; ML-KEM-768 Data Size 1184/1088 octets; ML-KEM-1024 Payload Length 1576/1576; ML-KEM-1024 Data Size 1568/1568 octets; 32-byte shared secret
- **Target Audience**: Developer; Security Architect
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Support for RFC 9370 Multiple Key Exchanges; Path MTU discovery for ML-KEM-768/1024; Independent randomness generation for ML-KEM seeds
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: vpn-ssh-pqc; hybrid-crypto; Algorithms; Threats
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: Downgrade attacks; malformed public keys; manipulated ciphertexts; implicit rejection failures
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: None detected
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: Input validation checks per FIPS 203 Section 7.2 and 7.3
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: None detected
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: Independent randomness seeds; no reuse of randomness in KEM ciphertext generation

## draft-ietf-ipsecme-ikev2-pqc-auth

- **Reference ID**: draft-ietf-ipsecme-ikev2-pqc-auth
- **Title**: Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) Authentication in IKEv2
- **Authors**: IETF IPSECME
- **Publication Date**: 2024-07-01
- **Last Updated**: 2024-07-01
- **Document Status**: Internet Draft
- **Main Topic**: Specifies a generic mechanism for integrating post-quantum cryptographic digital signature algorithms, specifically ML-DSA and SLH-DSA, into the IKEv2 protocol for authentication.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-DSA, SLH-DSA
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Cryptographically Relevant Quantum Computer (CRQC)
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected

- **Key Takeaways**: Implement side-channel resistant ML-DSA variants for IKEv2 authentication; Use hedged signing modes to enhance security against side-channel attacks without impacting interoperability; Leverage RFC 9593 to announce supported PQC authentication methods for improved interoperability; Select PQC signature algorithms based on desired NIST security categories and performance requirements

## draft-ietf-lake-edhoc-psk

- **Reference ID**: draft-ietf-lake-edhoc-psk
- **Title**: draft-ietf-lake-edhoc-psk
- **Authors**: IETF
- **Publication Date**: 2026-03-02
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-02
- **Document Status**: Active
- **Main Topic**: Specification of a Pre-Shared Key (PSK) authentication method for the Ephemeral Diffie-Hellman Over COSE (EDHOC) key exchange protocol.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Cryptographically Relevant Quantum Computer (CRQC); passive quantum attacker; active quantum adversaries
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Elsa Lopez-Perez; Göran Selander; John Preuß Mattsson; Rafael Marin-Lopez; Francisco Lopez-Gomez
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: EDHOC; ECDHE; TLS 1.3; OSCORE; EAP; AKMA
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Key Management; Key Encapsulation Mechanism (KEM)
- **Standardization Bodies**: IETF; IANA
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: BCP 78; BCP 79; Revised BSD License
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman (ECDH); ECDHE; AEAD
- **Key Takeaways**: EDHOC-PSK provides quantum-resistant authentication and key exchange when using a symmetric PSK, even with ECDHE; ECDHE contribution degenerates to randomness only against a CRQC, losing PFS and identity protection; Combining EDHOC-PSK with a quantum-resistant KEM like ML-KEM ensures identity protection and PFS against quantum attackers; The protocol supports cryptographic agility and downgrade protection through cipher suite negotiation; Compromise of the long-term PSK allows full active impersonation in all future sessions as KCI protection is not provided
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: None detected
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Combining EDHOC-PSK with a quantum-resistant Key Encapsulation Mechanism (KEM); cryptographic agility; modularity and negotiation of preferred cryptographic primitives

- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Algorithms; crypto-agility; iot-ot-pqc; hybrid-crypto; quantum-threats

- **Reference ID**: draft-ietf-lamps-cms-kyber-13

- **Authors**: IETF LAMPS
- **Publication Date**: 2023-01-10
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-01
- **Document Status**: Published (RFC 9936)
- **Main Topic**: Specification of conventions for using ML-KEM with the Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) via the KEMRecipientInfo structure defined in RFC 9629.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM, ML-KEM-512, ML-KEM-768, ML-KEM-1024
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Post-Quantum
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Years mentioned: 2025, 2026

- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: SHA3-256, SHA3-512, SHAKE128, SHAKE256, HKDF, HMAC, SHA-256, AES-Wrap-128, AES-Wrap-256, AES-192
- **Key Takeaways**: ML-KEM parameter sets map to 128-bit, 192-bit, and 256-bit security strengths; Implementations must support HKDF with SHA-256 for key derivation; AES-Wrap-128 is required for ML-KEM-512 while AES-Wrap-256 is required for ML-KEM-768 and ML-KEM-1024; The document specifies direct use of ML-KEM in CMS but does not preclude hybrid schemes; S/MIME implementations may announce ML-KEM support via the SMIMECapabilities attribute.

- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-DSA
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected

- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: FIPS 204
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: None detected
- **Key Takeaways**: Conforming CA implementations must use specific NIST-registered OIDs for ML-DSA signatures in certificates and CRLs; The parameters component for ML-DSA algorithm identifiers must be absent; Only the pure variant of ML-DSA is specified, excluding the pre-hash variant; ML-DSA public keys are encoded as raw byte strings within the subjectPublicKey BIT STRING field; The seed format is recommended for encoding ML-DSA private keys outside of an Asymmetric Key Package.

- **Reference ID**: draft-ietf-lamps-dilithium-certificates-13
- **Title**: Internet X.509 PKI: Algorithm Identifiers for ML-DSA
- **Authors**: IETF LAMPS
- **Publication Date**: 2024-10-01
- **Last Updated**: 2024-10-01
- **Document Status**: Internet Draft
- **Main Topic**: Defines X.509 algorithm identifiers and parameter encoding conventions for using ML-DSA in Internet X.509 certificates and Certificate Revocation Lists.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-DSA
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected

- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected

- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: AES-128; AES-192; AES-256; SHA-256; SHA-384; SHAKE-256
- **Key Takeaways**: Implementers must use specific OBJECT IDENTifiers for ML-DSA-44, ML-DSA-65, and ML-DSA-87 with absent parameters; ML-DSA keys are restricted to digital signatures and non-repudiation, prohibiting key establishment or encryption usage; Implementations must ensure high-quality random number generation to prevent security property undermining; Side-channel countermeasures like masking are available but introduce performance overhead; The document specifies only the pure variant of ML-DSA, excluding the pre-hash variant.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: NIST security level 1; NIST security level 2; NIST security level 3; NIST security level 4; NIST security level 5; ML-DSA-44; ML-DSA-65; ML-DSA-87

## draft-ietf-lamps-kyber-certificates

- **Reference ID**: draft-ietf-lamps-kyber-certificates
- **Title**: Internet X.509 PKI — Algorithm Identifiers for ML-KEM
- **Authors**: IETF LAMPS WG
- **Publication Date**: 2023-07-01
- **Last Updated**: 2025-08-01
- **Document Status**: Internet-Draft (Standards Track)
- **Main Topic**: Specification of algorithm identifiers, key formats, and conventions for using ML-KEM in X.509 Public Key Infrastructure certificates.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: None detected
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected

- **Infrastructure Layers**: PKI
- **Standardization Bodies**: IETF, NIST
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: FIPS 203
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: None detected
- **Key Takeaways**: ML-KEM certificates require specific OIDs assigned by NIST for algorithm identification; The parameters field in the AlgorithmIdentifier for ML-KEM public keys must be absent; Key usage bits must be set to keyEncipherment for ML-KEM certificates; Private keys can be stored as a 64-byte seed, an expanded key, or both to support interoperability; ML-KEM public key sizes are 800, 1184, and 1568 octets for security levels 512, 768, and 1024 respectively
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ML-KEM-512, ML-KEM-768, ML-KEM-1024
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Pure PQC KEM Support**: None detected

Wood, Bas Westerbaan, Falko Strenzke, Piotr Popis, Jean-Pierre Fiset, Carl Wallace, Daniel Van Geest, Abel C. H. Chen, Austin Lin, Douglas Stebila (Contributors/Reviewers)
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected

- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ML-KEM-768, ML-KEM-1024, 256-bit shared secret key, 256-bit outputs
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Composite ML-KEM, PQ/T hybrid scheme, dual-algorithm security, migration flexibility, cryptographic agility, backwards compatibility

- **Reference ID**: draft-ietf-lamps-pq-composite-sigs-13
- **Title**: Composite ML-DSA for Use in X.509 PKI and CMS
- **Authors**: IETF LAMPS
- **Publication Date**: 2023-03-02
- **Last Updated**: 2025-10-29
- **Document Status**: Internet-Draft
- **Main Topic**: This document defines composite signature algorithms combining US NIST ML-DSA with traditional algorithms like RSA, ECDSA, and EdDSA for use in X.509 PKI and CMS applications.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-DSA
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Quantum attacks on traditional cryptographic signature algorithms; breaks or catastrophic bugs in ML-DSA
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Aggressive migration timelines may require deploying PQC algorithms before implementations are fully hardened or certified
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: United States (US NIST); Bodies: IETF, IESG, LAMPS WG, ANSSI, BSI, NLNCSA, Swedish National Communications Security Authority, Swedish Armed Forces, CA/Browser Forum
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Mike Ounsworth, John Gray, Massimiliano Pala, Jan Klaußner, Scott Fluhrer (Authors); Donald Eastlake (SECDIR Reviewer); Tim Evens (GENART Reviewer); Russ Housley (Document Shepherd); Deb Cooley (Responsible AD)
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: X.509, PKIX, TLS 1.3, IKEv2, S/MIME, CMS
- **Infrastructure Layers**: PKI, Public Key Infrastructure, Trust Store, Certificate Chain
- **Standardization Bodies**: IETF, US NIST, ITU-T, American National Standards Institute, CA/Browser Forum
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: FIPS 204, BCP 78, BCP 79, BCP 14, RFC 9794, European eIDAS regulation
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA, RSASSA-PKCS1-v1.5, RSASSA-PSS, ECDSA, Ed25519, Ed448, DSA, SHA2, SHA3, SHAKE256
- **Key Takeaways**: Composite ML-DSA provides extra protection against breaks or catastrophic bugs in ML-DSA by requiring an adversary to break all component algorithms simultaneously; Organizations should deploy a single or small number of composite algorithms tailored to their environment rather than implementing the entire list; The specification does not provide application backwards compatibility, requiring upgraded systems to understand the new OIDs; Timing side-channels may leak which component signature algorithm failed if component primitives are not constant-time including error cases
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ML-DSA-44, ML-DSA-65, ML-DSA-87, Ed25519, Ed448, P-256, P-384, P-521
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Post-Quantum/Traditional (PQ/T) Hybrids, Composite signatures, Cryptographic agility, Stepping stone for transition

## draft-ietf-lamps-pq-composite-sigs-14

- **Reference ID**: draft-ietf-lamps-pq-composite-sigs-14
- **Title**: Composite ML-DSA for Use in X.509 PKI and CMS
- **Authors**: IETF LAMPS
- **Publication Date**: 2023-03-02
- **Last Updated**: 2026-01-07
- **Document Status**: Internet-Draft (In IETF Last Call)
- **Main Topic**: Specification of composite digital signature algorithms combining ML-DSA with traditional algorithms for use in X.509 PKI and CMS to facilitate hybrid post-quantum migration.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-DSA
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Quantum attacks on traditional cryptographic signature algorithms; Harvest Now Decrypt Later implied by migration timelines
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Expires 28 August 2026; aggressive migration timelines may require deploying PQC algorithms before fully hardened
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Mike Ounsworth, John Gray, Massimiliano Pala, Jan Klaußner, Scott Fluhrer (Authors); Donald Eastlake (SECDIR review); Tim Evens (GENART review); Russ Housley (Document shepherd); Deb Cooley (Action Holder/Responsible AD)
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: X.509, PKIX, CMS, PKCS#10, CMP, TLS 1.3, IKEv2, S/MIME
- **Infrastructure Layers**: PKI, Public Key Infrastructure
- **Standardization Bodies**: IETF, IESG, LAMPS Working Group, ITU-T, ISO, European Parliament and Council
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: eIDAS regulations, CA/Browser Forum Baseline Requirements, BCP 78, BCP 79, Revised BSD License
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA, DSA, ECDSA, Ed25519, Ed448, SHA-2, SHA-3, SHAKE256, SHA-256, SHA-512
- **Key Takeaways**: Composite ML-DSA allows drop-in replacement for existing signature algorithms in X.509 and PKIX protocols; Hybrid schemes hedge against security vulnerabilities in new PQC implementations; Protocol backwards compatibility is achieved without modifying protocol specifications; Multiple hash functions may be required within a single composite algorithm to maintain security levels; Prefix values can mitigate attacks by preventing splitting of composite signatures into components
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ML-DSA-65, ML-DSA-87, ML-DSA-1024, Ed25519, Ed448, secp256r1
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Post-Quantum/Traditional (PQ/T) Hybrids, Composite signatures, Protocol backwards compatibility, Drop-in replacement for existing digital signature algorithms

- **Hybrid PQC KEM Support**: None detected

## draft-ietf-lamps-pq-composite-sigs-15

- **Reference ID**: draft-ietf-lamps-pq-composite-sigs-15
- **Title**: Composite ML-DSA for Use in X.509 PKI and CMS
- **Authors**: IETF LAMPS
- **Publication Date**: 2023-03-02
- **Last Updated**: 2026-02-24
- **Document Status**: Internet-Draft (In IETF Last Call)
- **Main Topic**: This document defines composite ML-DSA signatures combining traditional algorithms with ML-DSA for use in X.509 PKI and CMS to provide hybrid protection against quantum attacks.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-DSA
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Quantum computing threats to traditional cryptographic signature algorithms; breaks or catastrophic bugs in ML-DSA
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Years mentioned: 2025, 2026; Keywords: deprecat
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: Certain regions with regulatory guidelines; governments and cybersecurity agencies including BSI and ANSSI
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Mike Ounsworth, John Gray, Massimiliano Pala, Jan Klaußner, Scott Fluhrer (Authors); Russ Housley (Document shepherd); Deb Cooley (Action Holder/Responsible AD); Donald Eastlake (SECDIR review); Tim Evens (GENART review)

- **Infrastructure Layers**: Public Key Infrastructure (PKI), X.509 data structures

- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSASSA-PKCS1-v1.5, RSASSA-PSS, ECDSA, Ed25519, Ed448, RSA, DSA
- **Key Takeaways**: Composite signatures combine ML-DSA with traditional algorithms to ensure security even if one component is broken; Hybrid schemes allow deployment of PQC before full certification or audit is complete; The composite approach provides protocol backwards compatibility by presenting a single atomic algorithm; Organizations should select specific composite combinations based on operational constraints rather than implementing all options; Certain jurisdictions recommend exclusive use of ML-DSA within a PQ/T hybrid framework
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: EUF-CMA level security; SUF-CMA; Non-separability; FIPS 204 parameter sets implied but not explicitly listed as specific sizes in text
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Composite signatures (PQ/T Hybrids); Protocol backwards compatibility; Stepping stone for transition from deployed cryptographic algorithms
